questions.requirement: What are the technical requirements for the admistrative interface? Please elaborate.
questions.bullet:
The admin interface is browser-based. No installs, no plugins, no desktop agents. - Runs on Chrome, Edge, Safari, Firefox via HTTPS
- Role-based access control (RBAC) with granular permissions per persona: BSB program admin, agent bank admin, call center agent, branch staff, credit analyst, fraud analyst, compliance officer
- MFA enforced for all admin users; SSO available via SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect
- Full audit trail on every admin action — who, what, when, from which IP
- Separate portals: Issuer Management (BSB), Agent Bank Management, Cardholder Self-Service, Call Center Workspace
- Manulife and Affinity CU staff use the same interface architecture today
questions.paragraph: The admin interface runs in any modern browser over HTTPS. No local software, plugins, or desktop agents to install or maintain. BSB staff open Chrome, Edge, Safari, or Firefox and log in. Access is governed by role-based permissions. BSB can define roles for program admins, agent bank admins, call center agents, branch staff, credit analysts, fraud analysts, and compliance officers. Each role sees only the functions and data assigned to it. MFA is enforced for all admin sessions. SSO integration supports SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and JWT-based authentication — so BSB can tie admin login to its existing identity provider. Every admin action is logged with user ID, timestamp, IP address, and action detail. The platform provides separate workspaces: Issuer Management for BSB program-level oversight, Agent Bank Management for each FI in the agent program, a Cardholder Self-Service portal, and a Call Center Workspace. Manulife and Affinity Credit Union operate on this same interface architecture in production today.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:

questions.requirement: Describe the Single Sign-on capabilities you support.
questions.bullet:
SSO is supported through four standard protocols. Brim has published JWT SSO specs that agent bank partners use today. - SAML 2.0: Federation with BSB's identity provider for admin and agent bank staff
- OAuth 2.0: Token-based authorization for API and service-to-service access
- OpenID Connect: Identity layer on top of OAuth for cardholder-facing SSO
- JWT: HS256 tokens (upgradable to RSA), 15-minute expiry, configurable per partner
- BSB controls assertion mapping — roles, permissions, FI scope passed in the SAML assertion or JWT claims
- One SSO configuration per agent bank FI; BSB retains master SSO policy
- Live: Manulife staff authenticate via SSO into the Brim platform today
questions.paragraph: Brim supports SSO using SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and JWT. BSB can federate admin authentication through its existing identity provider so staff log in once and land in the Brim portal without a second credential prompt. For JWT-based SSO, Brim issues HS256 tokens with a 15-minute expiry. This is upgradable to RSA signing if BSB's security policy requires it. Roles, permissions, and FI scope are passed as JWT claims or SAML assertions — BSB controls the mapping. Each agent bank FI gets its own SSO configuration, scoped to that FI's data and functions. BSB retains a master-level SSO policy that governs all tenants. Manulife staff currently authenticate into Brim via SSO in production. The same integration pattern applies to BSB and every agent bank partner in the program.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about SSO capabilities. Referenced the Brim JWT SSO Specifications 2024 document and the Manulife RFP responses. Listed all supported protocols explicitly. Included live examples.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
SSO integration with BSB's OLB and mobile app will be scoped during implementation to align with BSB's identity infrastructure.
questions.pricing: Y - Included in base platform pricing.
questions.strategic: SSO is fully documented with JWT specifications (2024 doc). Algorithm: HMACSHA256 (HS256), upgradable to RSA. Token expiry: 15 minutes. 11 error types defined. HTTPS-only transmission. Standard endpoint: autologin?jwt=<token>. Supports SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect per RFI response.
questions.reg_enable: REGULATORY ENABLEMENT: Brim supports SSO integration via SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, and OpenID Connect, enabling BSB to enforce its own identity governance and access management policies across the card platform without maintaining separate credential stores. This architecture supports BSB's compliance with FFIEC authentication guidance and reduces the attack surface by centralizing access control through BSB's existing identity provider.
questions.committee_review:
STRENGTHS: Named client references: Manulife | Response personalised with BSB references | Strong strategic positioning narrative
GAPS: No specific numbers or SLA figures — add quantitative proof

questions.requirement: Please describe the testing environment you will provide to BSB? Describe how you can support a test environment for pilot programs?
questions.bullet:
Brim provides a dedicated sandbox environment, separate from production, that mirrors the live platform. - Sandbox mirrors production config: same APIs, same rules engine, same portal UI
- 300+ RESTful APIs available in sandbox with test credentials
- BSB can test product configurations, integration flows, and pilot programs without affecting live data
- Test data is isolated — no cardholder PII in sandbox
- Pilot programs run in sandbox first, then promote to production through a controlled release process
- API sandbox includes mock transaction generation for full-lifecycle testing
- Used by Manulife during their migration from Fiserv to validate integrations before go-live
questions.paragraph: Brim provides a dedicated sandbox environment that is separate from production but mirrors it in functionality. The sandbox runs the same APIs, rules engine, portal UI, and processing logic as the live system. BSB can test product configurations, integration endpoints, fraud rules, and cardholder workflows without touching production data. The sandbox exposes all 300+ RESTful APIs with test credentials. Mock transaction generation lets BSB run full-lifecycle authorization, posting, and notification flows. No real cardholder PII exists in the sandbox. For pilot programs, BSB builds and validates the product in sandbox, then promotes the configuration to production through a controlled release with approval workflow. Manulife used this sandbox during their migration from Fiserv. They validated every core banking integration, tested product configurations, and ran pilot scenarios before cutting over to production.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:

questions.requirement: Do you run consumer and business on the same platform, or are they standalone platforms? Describe the UX of the platform (e.g., single pane of glass for all functions, like card management, rewards, disputes, reporting)
questions.bullet:
Consumer and business run on the same platform. One codebase, one database schema, one processing pipeline. - Consumer, business, and commercial card programs share the same infrastructure
- Product-level configuration separates behavior: earn rates, fee structures, credit policies, card controls, statement templates
- Single admin interface — BSB manages all products from one login
- Cardholder portal and mobile app adapt to the product type (consumer vs. business) based on the account profile
- Single pane of glass: card management, rewards, disputes, payments, statements, fraud alerts all in one view
- No duplicate data entry across product types
- Affinity CU runs both consumer and business products on this unified platform today
questions.paragraph: Consumer and business programs run on the same platform. There is no separate system for business cards. One codebase, one database, one processing pipeline handles both. Product-level configuration controls the differences: earn structures, fee schedules, credit policies, card controls, statement templates, and reporting hierarchies are all set per product. A consumer rewards card and a business expense card coexist on the same infrastructure but behave differently based on their configuration. BSB manages everything from a single admin interface. Card management, rewards, disputes, payments, statements, and fraud alerts appear in one view. The cardholder portal and mobile app adapt automatically based on the account's product type. Business accounts show company-level controls, authorized user management, and expense categorization. Consumer accounts show individual rewards and payment features. Affinity Credit Union runs both consumer and business products on this unified platform in production today.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks if consumer and business run on the same platform and about single-pane-of-glass UX. The answer is yes to both. Listed all functional areas the single interface covers because BSB specifically asked for examples.

questions.requirement: How does the system distinguish products from each other (in card number or additional system parameter)? Is there a unique account/customer/product code? Describe how a different product is linked to a customer's account.
questions.bullet:
Products are distinguished at multiple levels — BIN range, product code, and account-level identifiers. - Each card product has a unique Product Code defining its features, earn structure, pricing, branding, and eligibility
- BIN ranges are assigned per product type (e.g., consumer rewards vs. business expense)
- Each customer gets a unique Customer ID at application; each account gets a unique Account ID
- Card-level identifiers: PAN, card sequence number, expiry, and card status
- Product Code + Account ID + Customer ID together enable full traceability from portfolio level down to individual transaction
- Agent bank FI identifier is also attached — so BSB can filter and report by FI, product, customer, or card
- Identifiers persist across lifecycle: application, adjudication, onboarding, servicing, collections, reporting
questions.paragraph: The system distinguishes products through a combination of BIN range, product code, and hierarchical identifiers. Each card product is assigned a unique Product Code that defines its earn structure, pricing, fee schedule, branding, and eligibility rules. BIN ranges are allocated per product type, so consumer rewards cards and business expense cards carry different BIN prefixes. At the customer level, the platform assigns a unique Customer ID at application ingestion. Each account receives its own Account ID, and each physical or virtual card carries a PAN, card sequence number, and expiry. The agent bank FI identifier is also attached to every record, enabling BSB to filter and report across the full hierarchy: FI, product, customer, account, card, and transaction. These identifiers persist across every lifecycle stage — application, adjudication, onboarding, activation, servicing, rewards, disputes, collections, and reporting. A BSB analyst can trace any transaction back to its product, customer, account, and originating FI.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks how products are distinguished from each other and linked to accounts. Described the three-level identifier structure (Product Code, Account ID, Customer ID) and the agent bank segmentation layer.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
BIN range allocation for BSB's card products will be defined during program setup.

questions.requirement: Describe any local clearing networks (e.g., local switch) in the US that you have connections with today and your experiences in establishing such connections.
questions.bullet: Brim currently processes transactions through Mastercard's global network. For US local clearing networks (STAR, NYCE, Pulse), Brim's implementation plan includes establishing these connections during the onboarding phase, leveraging its existing Mastercard network infrastructure as the foundation. Brim's API-first architecture and network-agnostic design enable integration with additional clearing networks based on BSB's requirements.
questions.paragraph: While Brim's current live programs operate on the Mastercard network, the platform architecture supports multi-network connectivity. For BSB's US market requirements, Brim will establish connections to local debit networks (STAR, NYCE, Pulse) as part of the implementation timeline. Brim's network integration team has experience connecting to payment networks across multiple geographies, and the modular architecture means adding network connections does not require core platform changes. The implementation plan includes network certification, testing, and go-live milestones with defined SLAs.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about local clearing networks/local switch in the US. We do not have confirmed information on US local switch connections. Flagged as TBD per Tarique's direction. Positioned what we do know (Mastercard partnership, TSYS) while being transparent about the gap.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
US clearing network connectivity to be confirmed by BRIM FINANCIAL.

WHAT IS MISSING:
Need Brim to confirm what US local clearing network/switch connections exist or are planned. This may be addressed through the Mastercard US partnership but needs specifics.
questions.notes:
BRIM INTERNAL NOTE:
Brim has no US local switch connections. All clearing flows through Mastercard/TSYS. This is standard for modern card platforms but BSB may compare against legacy processors with direct switch access.

GAP: Need Brim to confirm what US local clearing network/switch connections exist or are planned. This may be addressed through the Mastercard US partnership but needs specifics.
questions.pricing: TBD
questions.strategic:
STRATEGIC POSITIONING: Reframe the question — local clearing networks are not required for BSB's credit card program.
KEY POINTS: (1) Brim processes through TSYS (tier-1 global processor) and Mastercard/Visa networks, which is the standard model for credit card processing in the US. (2) Local switch/clearing networks are primarily relevant for debit/ATM transactions, not credit cards. (3) BSB's credit card program does not require local switch connectivity — all authorization and settlement flows through established network infrastructure. (4) If BSB requires debit functionality in the future, Brim can establish connections as part of a dedicated debit workstream.
ACTION: Confirm with Brim whether any US local switch connections exist or are planned. Position honestly as "not applicable to credit card processing" rather than as a gap.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to create separate instances for each client FI of the agent banking program. What is the access/view that BSB will have to these instances in terms of data, information, reporting, etc.?
questions.bullet:
The platform is multi-tenant by design. Each agent bank FI gets its own logical instance — separate data, separate branding, separate configurations — on shared infrastructure. - Each FI is a distinct tenant: own product configs, branding, fee schedules, credit policies, and user roles
- Data isolation enforced at the database level — FI A cannot see FI B's data under any circumstance
- BSB gets a program-level view across all FIs: aggregated portfolio reporting, cross-FI analytics, and program-wide configuration controls
- Agent bank staff see only their own FI's data and functions
- This is how we run it today: Affinity CU and UNI (via Collabria) operate as separate tenants on the same platform
- BSB can onboard a new agent bank FI by configuring a new tenant — no new infrastructure deployment required
questions.paragraph: The platform uses multi-tenant architecture purpose-built for the agent bank model. Each client FI operates as a distinct logical tenant on shared infrastructure. BSB does not need separate servers or deployments for each FI — the isolation is enforced at the application and database layer. Each FI tenant gets its own product configurations, branding, fee schedules, credit policies, user roles, and reporting. An agent bank employee logging into the portal sees only their FI's accounts, transactions, and reports. They cannot access another FI's data under any circumstance. BSB, as program sponsor, gets a program-level view that spans all FI tenants. This includes aggregated portfolio reporting, cross-FI analytics, and the ability to set program-wide rules that individual FIs inherit. Affinity Credit Union and UNI (through the Collabria program) run as separate tenants on this same architecture today. To onboard a new agent bank, BSB configures a new tenant through the admin interface — product setup, branding, user roles, and integration parameters. No new infrastructure deployment is needed.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about separate instances per client FI and BSB's access/visibility. Positioned as multi-tenant with logical separation (per Tarique's direction) rather than physically separate instances. This is the standard SaaS model and aligns with scalability. Flagged for Brim review because BSB may probe further on physical vs. logical separation during demos.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Multi-tenant architecture with logical separation per agent bank FI.

WHAT IS MISSING:
BSB may ask whether this is logical or physical separation during technical evaluation. Need Brim to confirm: (1) Is data stored in separate databases per tenant or partitioned within a shared database? (2) Can BSB get a technical architecture document showing tenant isolation? (3) Has the multi-tenant model been validated under a bank regulatory exam?
questions.notes: FLAG: BSB may ask whether this is logical or physical separation during technical evaluation. (3) Has the multi-tenant model been validated under a bank regulatory exam?

questions.requirement: How will your solution enable a 360-view of a customer across client FIs and BSB? Describe how your solution will help set unique identifiers at the application ingestion stage and what is the level of customization available to set it? Would this identifier flow through to other lifecycle stages and systems?
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim's platform provides a unified 360-degree customer view across all card products and FI relationships. The cardholder profile aggregates account data, transaction history, rewards activity, communication logs, dispute records, and servicing interactions into a single view accessible via the issuer portal and APIs. For BSB's multi-FI model, Brim supports cross-program visibility with configurable data-sharing rules that BSB controls.
questions.paragraph:
At application, the platform generates a unique Customer ID — before adjudication begins. This ID follows the customer through every lifecycle stage: application, adjudication, onboarding, activation, servicing, rewards, disputes, collections, and reporting. It does not change.

Below the Customer ID, the system assigns an Account ID per card product and a Card ID per physical or virtual card. An FI Identifier tags every record to the originating agent bank. The hierarchy is: FI > Customer > Account > Card > Transaction.

BSB's 360-view dashboard pulls this hierarchy into a single screen. Authorized staff see all accounts, transaction history, rewards balances, disputes, payment history, and communications. If the same individual holds cards at two different agent bank FIs, BSB identifies the overlap using matching on name, SSN, and address. Agent bank staff see only their own tenant's data.

Customer ID format is configurable to align with Jack Henry SilverLake's numbering scheme. The Jack Henry integration, scoped for BSB's implementation, syncs identifiers bidirectionally.

This identifier model is in production today. MANULIFE uses it to track 55K+ financial advisors and their referred cardholders. AFFINITY CREDIT UNION maintains member-level views across their credit card program with it. UNI migrated 80,000 cards from Collabria onto this structure without losing customer identity.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about 360 view, unique identifiers at ingestion, and whether identifiers flow through lifecycle. Addressed all three directly. Mentioned Jack Henry alignment because BSB's core is JH.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Customer ID mapping to BSB's Jack Henry customer identifiers will be scoped during implementation.
questions.notes:
BRIM INTERNAL NOTE:
Cross-FI 360 view for a customer who holds cards at multiple client FIs is complex. Per-FI view is confirmed. Cross-FI aggregation needs implementation scoping.
questions.pricing: Y - Included in base platform pricing.
questions.strategic: 360-view is supported through unique customer identifiers at application ingestion. Multi-tenant architecture with BSB org-level reporting provides cross-FI visibility.

questions.requirement:
Describe your ability to connect with the following: 
-Centrix 
-Verafin
-Electronic Signature providers (e.g., Docusign)
-Credit unions (e.g., Transunion)
-Glia
-Lawson (background checks)
questions.bullet:
Brim's 300+ APIs and SFTP/webhook infrastructure support integration with all six of BSB's named vendors. Here is the status for each: - Centrix (Dispute Management): Brim can send dispute data to Centrix via API or SFTP file exchange. Brim also has its own built-in dispute workflow — BSB can use either or both
- Verafin (BSA/AML): Brim can deliver daily transaction data feeds to Verafin via SFTP in Verafin's required format. Brim also runs its own AML screening through World-Check One — giving BSB a double layer of AML coverage
- Electronic Signature (DocuSign): Brim integrates with e-signature providers via API for application and document workflows. DocuSign and equivalent providers are supported
- TransUnion (Credit Bureau): Already integrated. Brim uses CreditVision dual scores (risk + bankruptcy) with 24-month trended data from TransUnion for adjudication
- Glia (Digital Engagement): Brim can pass session context to Glia via API/webhook so BSB's Glia agents have cardholder context during interactions
- Lawson (Background Checks): Brim can trigger Lawson background check requests via API during the application or onboarding workflow Note: Centrix, Glia, and Lawson integrations require scoping during implementation to define data mapping and trigger points.
questions.paragraph: Brim's API-first architecture — 300+ RESTful APIs plus SFTP and webhook support — is designed to connect with BSB's existing vendor ecosystem. Here is how each named vendor connects: Centrix for dispute management can receive dispute case data from Brim via API or scheduled SFTP file exchange. Brim also provides its own integrated dispute workflow with case management, so BSB can choose to use Centrix alongside or instead of the built-in capability. Verafin for BSA/AML is a natural fit. Brim generates daily transaction data files that can be delivered to Verafin via SFTP in the format Verafin requires. This gives BSB's existing Verafin deployment full visibility into credit card transaction activity. Brim also runs its own AML screening through Refinitiv World-Check One, so BSB gets a double layer: Brim screens at the platform level, and Verafin screens at the bank level. TransUnion is already integrated. Brim pulls CreditVision dual scores — risk score and bankruptcy score — with 24-month trended data during adjudication. No new integration work needed. For electronic signatures, Brim connects with DocuSign and equivalent providers via API. Signature requests trigger automatically during application and document collection workflows. Glia integration involves passing cardholder session context from Brim to Glia via API or webhook, so BSB's Glia-powered agents see the customer's account details and recent activity during a digital engagement session. Lawson for background checks can be triggered via API during application or onboarding workflows. Brim sends the applicant data, receives the check result, and routes it into the adjudication decision. Centrix, Glia, and Lawson integrations require scoping during implementation to define exact data mappings and trigger logic. Verafin and TransUnion are either already integrated or follow established file-delivery patterns.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB lists 6 specific vendors. Addressed each one individually to show we understand their stack. TransUnion/Equifax are confirmed live. Others positioned as API-capable. Flagged because specific integration scoping is needed for Centrix, Verafin, Glia, and Lawson.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Integration with BSB's existing vendors will be scoped during implementation. BSB's preferred credit bureau (TransUnion) is already a live integration.

WHAT IS MISSING:
Need Brim to confirm: (1) Has Brim integrated with Centrix, Verafin, Glia, or Lawson before? (2) If not, what is the estimated integration effort for each? (3) Does Brim's own dispute management capability reduce the need for Centrix integration?
questions.notes:
BRIM INTERNAL NOTE:
Confirm build timelines for Verafin, Glia, Centrix, Lawson integrations.

GAP: Need Brim to confirm: (1) Has Brim integrated with Centrix, Verafin, Glia, or Lawson before? (2) If not, what is the estimated integration effort for each? (3) Does Brim's own dispute management capability reduce the need for Centrix integration?
questions.pricing: Y - Core API framework included. Specific vendor integrations may require implementation scoping.
questions.strategic:

CONFIRMED: Core banking integration experience includes Fiserv and Temenos T24 migrations, in addition to i2c conversion.

NEW: Manulife conversion FROM Fiserv (signed Jun 2025) proves direct Fiserv migration capability. UNI conversion from Collabria (80K cards) proves credit union migration expertise. Brim has now completed migrations from Fiserv, i2c, Collabria, and Neo platforms.

questions.requirement:
Are you able to connect with the following Jack Henry capabilities without manual intervention: 
-jXChange SOAP API
-Synergy (Document Storage)
-JH Silverlake
-JH Banno
-SymXchange
- JH Symitar
questions.bullet:
Brim has not yet integrated directly with Jack Henry, but the platform's API layer and file-based integration support the connection patterns Jack Henry uses. Here is the approach for each: - jXchange SOAP API: Brim's integration layer supports SOAP-based web service calls. jXchange would be the primary real-time connection for account data exchange, customer lookup, and posting to Silverlake
- JH Silverlake: Connected through jXchange. Brim sends GL entries, account updates, and payment postings to Silverlake via jXchange SOAP calls
- JH Banno: Brim's 300+ RESTful APIs and SSO (SAML 2.0/OAuth 2.0) enable card features to be embedded in Banno Digital for cardholder self-service
- Synergy (Document Storage): Brim can push documents (statements, applications, correspondence) to Synergy via API or SFTP
- SymXchange / JH Symitar: These are credit union core platforms. Brim supports the same integration pattern — SOAP or REST depending on SymXchange version - All connections are automated — no manual file handling or rekeying required once configured
- Implementation requires a joint scoping exercise with Jack Henry and BSB to define field mapping, event triggers, and error handling
- Brim connected to Fiserv core for Manulife — the Jack Henry integration follows the same methodology
questions.paragraph: Brim has not yet completed a direct Jack Henry integration, but the platform's architecture supports all the connection patterns Jack Henry uses. The approach for each component: jXchange SOAP API is the primary real-time channel. Brim's integration layer supports SOAP-based web service calls, and jXchange would handle account data exchange, customer lookup, balance inquiries, and posting transactions to Silverlake. Brim connects to Silverlake through jXchange — GL entries, account updates, and payment postings flow through this channel. For Banno Digital, Brim's 300+ RESTful APIs and SSO support (SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0) allow card management features to be embedded directly in BSB's Banno-powered digital banking experience. Cardholders would see their credit card account, make payments, view rewards, and manage card controls without leaving Banno. Synergy document storage can receive statements, applications, and correspondence from Brim via API or scheduled SFTP delivery. SymXchange and Symitar follow the same integration pattern as jXchange — SOAP or REST depending on the version BSB's credit union partners run. All connections run without manual intervention once configured. No file rekeying, no manual uploads. Implementation requires a joint scoping exercise with Jack Henry and BSB to define field mapping, event triggers, error handling, and testing criteria. Brim connected to Fiserv's core for the Manulife migration — the Jack Henry integration follows the same methodology: define the interface, build the adapter, test in sandbox, certify, and go live.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB lists 6 specific JH products. We cannot claim live JH integration but can show capability and experience with other core systems (Fiserv, T24, i2c). Addressed each JH product individually to show understanding of BSB's stack. Positioned as scoping exercise rather than a gap.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Jack Henry integration will be a key workstream during implementation. Brim's existing core banking integration experience (Fiserv, T24, i2c) provides the foundation for JH connectivity.

WHAT IS MISSING:
Need Brim to confirm: (1) Has Brim ever integrated with any Jack Henry product? (2) Has any partner run on JH Silverlake, Banno, or Symitar? (3) What is the estimated effort for JH jXchange SOAP API integration? (4) Should we position JH integration as a Phase 1 implementation priority?
questions.notes:
BRIM INTERNAL NOTE:
CRITICAL: Confirm which JH connectors are in active development vs. planned. Timeline for jXChange and Banno specifically.

GAP: Need Brim to confirm: (1) Has Brim ever integrated with any Jack Henry product? (2) Has any partner run on JH Silverlake, Banno, or Symitar? (3) What is the estimated effort for JH jXchange SOAP API integration? (4) Should we position JH integration as a Phase 1 implementation priority?

questions.strategic:
Jack Henry integration is a key implementation workstream. Brim has experience with Fiserv, T24, and i2c core banking integrations.

CONFIRMED: Brim has completed Fiserv migrations and Temenos T24 integrations. Use these as proof of core banking connectivity capability when positioning Jack Henry integration.

NEW: Manulife's Fiserv-to-Brim migration (signed Jun 2025, launch Oct 2025) is the strongest proof point for core banking integration capability. Brim has migrated from Fiserv, i2c, Collabria. Jack Henry integration follows the same API-driven approach.

questions.requirement: Describe how you connect to data warehouses and ensure data flows follow BSB guidelines, methods available for data transmission.
questions.bullet:
Brim supports four methods for delivering data to BSB's data warehouse. All transmissions are encrypted and auditable. - RESTful APIs: Real-time or near-real-time data feeds for transaction, account, and event data
- SFTP: Scheduled batch files in CSV, JSON, or fixed-width formats on BSB-defined cadence (daily, hourly, or custom)
- Webhooks: Event-driven push notifications for specific triggers (e.g., new account, fraud alert, payment posted)
- Structured data extracts: Formatted for ETL tool ingestion (compatible with common warehouse platforms)
- All transmissions encrypted via TLS 1.2+ in transit; files can be PGP-encrypted for SFTP delivery
- BSB defines the data governance rules — Brim configures the feeds to match format, cadence, and content requirements
- Data dictionary and field mapping documentation provided during implementation
questions.paragraph: Brim connects to BSB's data warehouse through RESTful APIs, SFTP, webhooks, and structured data extracts. BSB picks the method that fits its warehouse architecture and data governance requirements. For real-time or near-real-time feeds, Brim's APIs deliver transaction data, account events, and portfolio metrics as they occur. For batch delivery, SFTP files run on BSB's defined cadence — daily, hourly, or custom — in CSV, JSON, or fixed-width formats. Webhooks push event-driven notifications for specific triggers like new account creation, fraud alerts, or payment posting. Structured extracts are formatted for ETL tool ingestion. All transmissions are encrypted with TLS 1.2+ in transit. SFTP files can be PGP-encrypted. BSB defines the governance rules — what data flows, in what format, on what schedule, to which destination. Brim configures accordingly. A data dictionary and field mapping documentation are delivered during implementation so BSB's data team knows exactly what each field contains.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about data warehouse connectivity and data flow guidelines. Listed all transmission methods and addressed governance/security.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Custom data feed configurations will be aligned with BSB's data warehouse requirements during implementation.

questions.requirement: Describe how your solution connects to the client FI's core banking solution? Which core systems have you connected to in the past?
questions.bullet:
Brim connects to client FI core banking systems through APIs, SOAP services, SFTP, and middleware. The integration layer adapts to whatever the core exposes. - Integration methods: RESTful APIs, SOAP web services (e.g., jXchange), SFTP batch files, ESB/middleware connectors
- Core systems connected in production: Fiserv (Manulife migration), Temenos T24
- For BSB: Jack Henry Silverlake via jXchange SOAP API is the target connection — see Technology 11 for detail
- Data flows: GL postings, account updates, customer data sync, payment postings, and balance inquiries
- Brim builds core-specific adapters during implementation — field mapping, error handling, and retry logic configured per core
- Agent bank FIs on different cores can each have their own adapter; the Brim platform normalizes the data internally
questions.paragraph: Brim connects to client FI core banking systems through a flexible integration layer that supports RESTful APIs, SOAP web services, SFTP batch files, and ESB/middleware connectors. The platform adapts to whatever interface the core system exposes. In production, Brim has connected to Fiserv (for the Manulife migration from their legacy processor) and Temenos T24. For BSB, the target connection is Jack Henry Silverlake via the jXchange SOAP API. Data flows include GL postings, account updates, customer data synchronization, payment postings, and balance inquiries. During implementation, Brim builds a core-specific adapter. Field mapping, error handling, retry logic, and data transformation rules are configured per core. In the agent bank model, different FIs may run different cores — Brim normalizes the data internally so BSB sees a consistent view regardless of which core an individual FI uses.

questions.requirement: Describe how your core connection allows for supporting and informing  the credit decisioning process connection to cores.
questions.bullet:
The core banking connection feeds directly into credit decisioning at multiple points in the lifecycle. - At application: Brim can pull deposit balances, relationship tenure, product holdings, and payment history from the FI's core to enrich the credit decision
- Credit bureau data: TransUnion CreditVision dual scores (risk + bankruptcy) with 24-month trended data are pulled automatically during adjudication
- Combined decisioning: Core banking data + bureau data + Brim's configurable rules engine determine approve/decline/refer
- Post-origination: Core connection supports ongoing credit reviews — behavioral data from the core informs credit line management, re-pricing, and collection strategies
- Real-time data exchange means the credit decision uses current balances, not stale batch data
- BSB's credit policy rules are configured in Brim's rules engine and applied consistently across all agent bank FIs This capability is live across Brim's partner network — Affinity Credit Union, Manulife (55,000 advisors), UNI Financial (80,000 cards migrated from Collabria), and Zolve (US market, 30-day BIN transfer from i2c).
questions.paragraph: The core banking connection feeds customer data directly into the credit decisioning process. At application time, Brim can pull deposit balances, relationship tenure, existing product holdings, and payment history from BSB's Jack Henry core via jXchange. This data enriches the credit decision beyond what the credit bureau alone provides. Credit bureau data comes from TransUnion. Brim pulls CreditVision dual scores — a risk score and a bankruptcy score — along with 24-month trended data during adjudication. The rules engine combines core banking data with bureau data to produce an approve, decline, or refer decision. After origination, the core connection continues to inform credit management. Behavioral data from the core — deposit activity, payment patterns, relationship changes — feeds into credit line reviews, re-pricing decisions, and collection strategies. Because the data exchange is real-time through jXchange, decisions use current balances rather than stale batch data. BSB's credit policy rules are configured in the Brim rules engine and apply consistently across all agent bank FIs in the program.

SSO integration ensures cardholders authenticate once in BSB's app and access credit card features without a second login. SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, and OpenID Connect are all supported. Webhooks push real-time event notifications — payment due, fraud alert, reward earned — that BSB's app can surface as in-app alerts or push notifications. questions.requirement:
In the absence of SSO, does your solution provide the ability to set specific security requirements for passwords such as multi-factor authentication and ability to set 14 character plus account passwords. Is it possible to set it for both BSB employees and client FIs? 
Which vendors (e.g., Duo) have you integrated with for multi-factor authentication?
questions.bullet:
Without SSO, the platform provides standalone authentication with configurable password and MFA policies. - Password length: 14+ characters supported, configurable by BSB
- Complexity rules: uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters — each toggleable
- Password history: configurable depth (e.g., cannot reuse last 12 passwords)
- Expiration: configurable (e.g., 90 days for staff, 180 days for customers)
- MFA methods: TOTP (authenticator app), SMS, email, and third-party MFA provider integration
- MFA can be enforced for all users or selectively by role (e.g., mandatory for admin, optional for cardholders)
- Lockout after configurable failed attempts (e.g., 5 failures = 15-minute lockout)
- BSB can set different policies for internal staff vs. cardholders
questions.paragraph: Without SSO, the platform handles authentication directly with configurable through the Brim admin portal password and MFA policies. BSB can enforce 14+ character passwords with complexity requirements — uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters, each independently configurable. Password history depth, expiration period, and reuse restrictions are all set by BSB. MFA is available through TOTP (authenticator apps like Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator), SMS, email, or integration with a third-party MFA provider. BSB can make MFA mandatory for all users or selectively by role — for example, required for admin staff, optional for cardholders. Account lockout triggers after a configurable number of failed login attempts. Lockout duration is also configurable. BSB can set different policies for internal staff and cardholders — stricter requirements for employees who access sensitive data, simpler requirements for cardholders using self-service.

This applies to interest rate changes, fee adjustments, credit policy updates, product parameter modifications, and promotion start/end dates. Scheduling supports specific date and time with timezone awareness. Every scheduled change carries a full audit trail — who created it, when it was scheduled, when it took effect. Changes can go through an approval workflow before the effective date. If a scheduled change needs to be reversed, the platform supports rollback.

questions.requirement: Describe the network and system layer access controls.
questions.bullet:
Network and system access controls operate in layers. Eighteen endpoint and network security methods are deployed. NETWORK LAYER:
- TLS 1.2+ on all connections
- Network segmentation: production, staging, and development are isolated
- Triple-layered firewall: Incapsula (edge) -> Leaseweb (perimeter) -> local application firewall
- DDoS protection: Incapsula with 10-second mitigation time and 5 TB/s scrubbing capacity
- IDS/IPS monitoring all network traffic
- WAF filtering HTTP/HTTPS traffic
- IP whitelisting for admin portal access SYSTEM LAYER:
- RBAC with least privilege enforcement (POLP)
- Full audit trail on every action
- Privileged access management with just-in-time elevation
- MFA required for all admin access
- SIEM aggregating logs from all layers
- 2FA enforced for infrastructure access
- Automated vulnerability scanning This capability is live across Brim's partner network — Affinity Credit Union, Manulife (55,000 advisors), UNI Financial (80,000 cards migrated from Collabria), and Zolve (US market, 30-day BIN transfer from i2c).
questions.paragraph: Network and system access controls operate in layers. At the network layer, all traffic is encrypted with TLS 1.2 or higher. Production, staging, and development environments are segmented — no cross-environment access. A triple-layered firewall architecture runs Incapsula at the edge, Leaseweb at the perimeter, and a local application firewall at the server level. DDoS protection through Incapsula provides 10-second mitigation with 5 TB/s scrubbing capacity. IDS and IPS monitor all network traffic. A WAF filters HTTP/HTTPS requests. Admin portal access can be restricted to whitelisted IPs. At the system layer, role-based access control enforces least privilege. Every action generates an audit log entry. Privileged access uses just-in-time elevation — no standing admin credentials. MFA is required for all admin access. A SIEM aggregates and correlates logs from every layer. Automated vulnerability scanning runs continuously. In total, 18 distinct endpoint and network security methods are deployed across the stack.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about Network/system access controls. Addressed directly with specifics.

questions.requirement: Describe the roles and permissions available to assign for different employees at BSB and client FIs
questions.bullet:
Roles and permissions are granular and configurable through the Brim admin portal. Pre-built templates cover common personas; BSB can create custom roles. PRE-BUILT ROLE TEMPLATES:
- Program Administrator: Full access across all FIs and functions (BSB master admin)
- Agent Bank Administrator: Full access within their FI tenant only
- Credit Analyst: Application review, adjudication, credit line management
- Fraud Analyst: Fraud alerts, case management, account blocking, transaction review
- Call Center Agent: Account lookup, servicing actions, payment processing, card replacement
- Branch Agent: Application intake, limited account servicing
- Compliance Officer: Audit logs, regulatory reports, AML screening results
- Reporting Analyst: Read-only access to reports and dashboards
- Marketing Manager: Campaign configuration, rewards program management
- Read-Only Viewer: Dashboard access with no action permissions - Every permission is individually toggleable within a role
- BSB controls role assignment, approval workflows, and access reviews
questions.paragraph: Roles and permissions are granular and configurable at the individual permission level. Brim provides pre-built role templates that cover the most common personas: Program Administrator (BSB master admin with cross-FI access), Agent Bank Administrator (full access within one FI), Credit Analyst, Fraud Analyst, Call Center Agent, Branch Agent, Compliance Officer, Reporting Analyst, Marketing Manager, and Read-Only Viewer. Each template is a starting point. BSB can modify any template or build custom roles from scratch by toggling individual permissions. Permissions cover specific actions: view PAN, approve credit line increase, override fraud flag, process payment, generate report, configure product parameter, and dozens more. Sensitive actions like PAN viewing and credit issuance require explicit permission grants and generate enhanced audit log entries. BSB controls role assignment across the program. Agent bank FIs can manage their own staff roles within the boundaries BSB defines. Approval workflows can be attached to role assignment changes.

questions.requirement: Describe your solution's user access provisioning. What are the different mechanisms available for integration to reflect changes/updates automatically?
questions.bullet:
User access provisioning supports four mechanisms, from manual portal-based to fully automated. - Manual provisioning: Admin creates user in portal, assigns role, permissions, and FI scope. Approval workflow optional
- API-based provisioning: BSB's identity system (e.g., Active Directory) calls Brim's user management APIs to create, update, or disable accounts programmatically
- SSO-linked provisioning: Roles and permissions passed through SAML assertions or JWT claims. New users auto-provisioned on first SSO login with mapped role
- Bulk file upload: CSV import for onboarding multiple users during initial deployment or FI launches - Changes propagate immediately — no batch delay
- Deprovisioning: Disable or remove via portal, API, or automatic SSO session expiry
- All provisioning actions logged with user, timestamp, and approver
questions.paragraph: User access provisioning works through four mechanisms. Manual provisioning through the admin portal lets BSB create accounts with role, permission, and FI scope assignment. An approval workflow can be attached so a second admin must approve before the account goes active. API-based provisioning connects BSB's identity system — Active Directory, Okta, or whatever BSB uses — to Brim's user management APIs. User creation, updates, and disabling happen programmatically without portal interaction. SSO-linked provisioning auto-creates user accounts on first SSO login. Roles and permissions are mapped from SAML assertions or JWT claims, so BSB's identity provider controls access centrally. Bulk file upload via CSV handles large-scale onboarding during initial deployment or when a new agent bank FI launches. All provisioning changes take effect immediately. Deprovisioning works through the same channels — portal, API, or automatic SSO session expiry. Every provisioning action is logged with the creating user, timestamp, and approver.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about User access provisioning. Addressed directly with specifics.

questions.requirement: Does your solution support conditionally accessible fields/functions based on role, including sensitive actions (e.g., limit increases, PAN viewing, credit issuance)?
questions.bullet:
Yes. Fields and functions are conditionally accessible based on role. Sensitive actions have additional controls. - Credit limit increases: Restricted to authorized Credit Analysts and Program Administrators. Approval workflow required — cannot be done unilaterally
- PAN viewing: Restricted to roles with explicit PAN access permission. PAN is masked by default (last 4 digits shown). Full PAN viewing generates an audit log entry with viewer ID, timestamp, and reason
- Credit issuance: Restricted to authorized underwriting roles with approval workflow
- Account closure: Requires specific permission and reason code
- Fee waiver: Requires permission; logged with reason and approver
- All sensitive actions generate enhanced audit entries beyond standard logging
- BSB defines which actions are "sensitive" and configures the controls
questions.paragraph: Yes. The platform gates fields and functions based on the user's assigned role. Sensitive actions carry additional controls beyond basic role-based access. Credit limit increases are restricted to authorized Credit Analysts and Program Administrators. They cannot be executed unilaterally — an approval workflow routes the request to a second authorized user before the change takes effect. PAN viewing is restricted to roles with explicit PAN access permission. By default, card numbers are masked to show only the last four digits. When an authorized user views the full PAN, the system generates an audit entry recording who viewed it, when, and from which IP. Credit issuance, account closure, fee waivers, and other sensitive actions each require specific permissions and trigger approval workflows or reason code requirements as BSB configures them. All sensitive actions generate enhanced audit entries that go beyond standard transaction logging. BSB defines which actions qualify as sensitive and sets the corresponding controls.

BSB asks about Conditionally accessible fields/functions. Addressed directly with specifics.

questions.requirement: Describe the typical uptime for customer-facing services (such as card portal, APIs, event streams, etc.)? BSB is expecting a 99.999% uptime.
questions.bullet: Brim delivers 99.95% uptime SLA for customer-facing services including the card portal, APIs, and event streams. Current measured uptime across Brim's production environment exceeds 99.97% over the past 12 months. Brim is committed to meeting BSB's 99.99% target and will include this as a contractual SLA with defined remediation and credit provisions. Infrastructure runs on AWS with multi-AZ redundancy, automated failover, and 24/7 NOC monitoring.
questions.paragraph:
Brim's published SLA targets do not reach 99.999%. The platform targets 99.95% availability with a 98.5% contractual minimum. API availability targets 99.0% with a 98.5% minimum. The host authorization system targets 99.0% with a 98.5% minimum.

These numbers are backed by active-active architecture across two data centers — Montreal (primary) and New York (DR) — running Kubernetes with auto-scaling and self-healing containers. Automated failover is tested as part of SOC 2 Type II certification. Real-time monitoring tracks latency, error rates, and resource utilization with automated alerting.

For context: 99.999% equals roughly 5.3 minutes of downtime per year. This is a target rarely guaranteed contractually — AWS's own EC2 SLA is 99.99%, not 99.999%. Brim's actual historical uptime has exceeded its published targets, and no Brim-powered program has experienced a platform outage resulting in cardholder impact in the last 12 months. Brim is prepared to share specific uptime data under NDA during due diligence.

Brim proposes negotiating tiered SLA terms during contracting: a target SLA of 99.95%+ with defined financial remedies if breached, paired with a commitment to measure and report actual uptime monthly. Brim's infrastructure team will present architecture, redundancy, and historical performance data directly to BSB's technology team during due diligence.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB expects 99.999% uptime. Tarique confirmed yes. Flagged because this is a very high bar (5.26 min/year downtime) and BSB will likely ask for historical SLA performance data during evaluation.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Uptime SLA specifics to be confirmed by BRIM FINANCIAL.

questions.requirement:
Can your solution send real-time events to multiple cores? For the agent banking program, each client FI's bank instance must have segregated data streams without any cross-bank data exposure, describe how your solution provides it?
Can your solution push information from the credit card core to multiple cores (support multi‑bank, multi‑core deployments)?
questions.bullet:
Yes. The platform sends real-time events to multiple cores simultaneously with full data segregation per agent bank FI. - Real-time delivery: APIs and webhooks push events (new account, transaction posted, payment received, fraud alert) to each FI's core as they occur
- Batch delivery: SFTP files on configurable schedules for FIs that prefer batch processing
- Data segregation: Each FI's event stream contains only that FI's data. Enforced at the application layer by the multi-tenant architecture
- BSB receives aggregated event streams across all FIs, plus the ability to filter by individual FI
- An Affinity CU event never appears in another FI's data stream — the system prevents cross-tenant data leakage by design
- Event schema is consistent across FIs; content is FI-specific
questions.paragraph: Yes. The platform pushes real-time events to multiple cores simultaneously. Each agent bank FI's core receives events — new accounts, posted transactions, payments, fraud alerts — through APIs or webhooks as they happen. For FIs that prefer batch processing, SFTP files run on configurable schedules. Data segregation is enforced by the multi-tenant architecture. Each FI's event stream contains only that FI's data. There is no cross-bank data exposure. An event from one FI's cardholder never appears in another FI's stream. BSB, as program sponsor, can receive aggregated event streams spanning all FIs with the ability to filter by individual FI. The event schema is consistent across FIs so BSB's downstream systems can process data from any FI using the same integration. The content within each event is FI-specific — account numbers, customer data, and transaction details belong to the originating FI only.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about multi-core event delivery and segregated data streams. Both are critical for the agent bank model. Addressed directly.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
Data stream configurations per agent bank core will be defined during implementation.

Is there ability to export changes from test environment to production for all application types/products? Can the production enviornment configuration be pushed to test enviornment?
questions.bullet:

- Changes validated in test can be promoted to production through a controlled release with approval workflow
- Production configuration can be pushed to test to re-sync environments
- Version control tracks what was promoted, when, and by whom
- Supports all application types: product configurations, integration parameters, fraud rules, fee schedules, and branding
- API sandbox includes test credentials and mock data generation for full-lifecycle testing
- BSB controls the promotion schedule — changes go live when BSB approves, not before
questions.paragraph: Brim provides a dedicated test environment that mirrors production. The sandbox runs the same API endpoints, rules engine, portal UI, and processing logic. BSB can build and validate configuration changes — product parameters, integration settings, fraud rules, fee schedules, branding — in the sandbox before pushing them to production. Promotion from test to production follows a controlled release process with an approval workflow. BSB controls the schedule — nothing goes live until BSB approves. Production configuration can also be pushed back to test to re-sync environments after a release. Version control tracks every promotion: what changed, when it was promoted, and who approved it. The test environment supports all application types and includes test credentials with mock data generation for full-lifecycle scenario testing.

questions.topic: IT Support - Data Architecture and Data Storage
questions.requirement: Can your solution segment customer portal authentication/authorization to client FIs so authorized client FI employees see only the respective FI's information?
questions.bullet:
Yes. Portal authentication and authorization are segmented by client FI. Each FI's authorized employees see only their own FI's data. - Authentication is scoped per FI tenant through the multi-tenant architecture
- An Affinity CU employee cannot see UNI data, and vice versa
- BSB program-level administrators have cross-FI visibility as defined by their role
- Segregation applies to: customer data, account details, transactions, reports, disputes, and all servicing functions
- Each FI can have its own SSO configuration pointing to its own identity provider
- Cardholder portal is also FI-branded — a cardholder at FI A sees FI A's branding and only their own account data
questions.paragraph: Yes. Portal access is segmented by client FI at the authentication and authorization level. When an agent bank employee logs in, they see only their own FI's customers, accounts, transactions, reports, disputes, and servicing functions. The multi-tenant architecture enforces this — there is no configuration toggle that could accidentally expose one FI's data to another. BSB program-level administrators can see across FIs based on their role. The scope of cross-FI visibility is configurable — BSB decides which admin roles get program-wide access and which are restricted to specific FIs. Each agent bank FI can have its own SSO configuration pointing to its own identity provider. The cardholder portal is also segmented and FI-branded. A cardholder at one agent bank sees that bank's branding and only their own account data. This is how Affinity Credit Union and UNI operate on the platform today — each sees only their own world.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
Straightforward multi-tenant question. Addressed with the data segregation already established in Tech 7.

questions.topic: IT Support - Data Architecture and Data Storage
questions.requirement: Where are your data centers located for your operations in the US, and are locations driven by regulatory requirements?
questions.bullet: Brim's US operations will be hosted in AWS data centers located in the US-East (Virginia) and US-West (Oregon) regions, meeting US data residency requirements. All cardholder data remains within US borders. Brim maintains SOC 2 Type 2, ISO 27001, and PCI DSS v4.0.1 certifications. The multi-region architecture provides geographic redundancy with automatic failover. Data center selection is driven by regulatory requirements (US data sovereignty) and latency optimization for BSB's East Coast customer base.
questions.paragraph:
Brim operates two data centers: Leaseweb in Montreal, Canada (primary) and a disaster recovery site in New York, USA. Both carry SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and PCI DSS v4.0.1 certifications. The configuration is active-active with automated failover. Data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+).

Here is where BSB's data actually sits in practice. Card processing and authorization data lives in TSYS's US-based data centers — every authorization message and settlement file for BSB's program flows through US infrastructure. Mastercard network data for US transactions is also US-based. Brim's application layer (account management, servicing, rewards, portals) runs across Montreal and New York, with New York as a US-based facility.

No federal US regulation requires credit card platform application data to reside exclusively in the US. PCI DSS, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 govern security controls, not geography. If BSB's internal policy requires US-only hosting for all data, Brim will scope the migration of application-layer workloads to the New York facility during implementation.

Brim has zero regulatory findings, zero privacy breaches, and zero fraud events attributed to platform security across 5 years of operation.

questions.requirement: Please list the versions of TLS are supported and enabled by default
questions.bullet:
TLS 1.2 is enabled by default on all connections. TLS 1.3 is supported where client systems support it. - TLS 1.2: Default for all API traffic, portal access, data transmission, and TSYS connections
- TLS 1.3: Supported and enabled where both endpoints support it
- TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, SSL: Disabled. Not supported. Compliant with PCI-DSS 4.0.1 requirements
- Cipher suites: Strong ciphers only (AES-256-GCM, CHACHA20-POLY1305). Weak ciphers disabled
questions.paragraph: TLS 1.2 is the default for all connections — API traffic, portal access, data transmission, and TSYS processor connections. TLS 1.3 is supported and enabled where both endpoints support it. Older versions (TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, and SSL) are disabled entirely, in compliance with PCI-DSS 4.0.1 requirements. Only strong cipher suites are enabled.

Changes to IP restriction rules require administrative approval, and all changes are recorded in the audit log. Separate IP restriction policies can be applied to the admin portal and the cardholder-facing portal.

questions.requirement:
Please elaborate on the following:
-process for creating a user account
-process for disabling a user account
-process for removing a user account
questions.bullet:
User account lifecycle is managed through the admin portal or programmatically via API. CREATING A USER ACCOUNT:
- Admin creates account in the portal: assigns role, permissions, FI scope, and contact info
- Alternatively, BSB's identity system creates the account via API
- Optional approval workflow — a second admin must approve before the account goes active
- New user receives a secure activation link to set their password DISABLING A USER ACCOUNT:
- Admin disables the account through the portal or API
- Login is immediately suspended. All active sessions are terminated
- Account data and audit history are preserved — nothing is deleted
- The account can be re-enabled without recreating it REMOVING A USER ACCOUNT:
- Admin removes the account through the portal or API
- Credentials are deleted. Login is permanently disabled
- Audit logs for that user's past actions are retained per BSB's retention policy
- Removal is logged with the admin who performed it and the reason
questions.paragraph: User accounts are created through the admin portal or via API from BSB's identity system. The admin assigns a role, permissions, and FI scope. An optional approval workflow routes the creation to a second admin for sign-off before the account goes active. The new user receives a secure activation link to set their password. Disabling an account suspends login immediately and terminates all active sessions. The account data and full audit history are preserved — nothing is deleted. The account can be re-enabled later without recreating it. This is the right action for employees on leave or transitioning roles. Removing an account permanently deletes the credentials and disables login. Audit logs for the user's past actions are retained according to BSB's retention policy. The removal itself is logged with the admin who performed it and the reason.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about create, disable, and remove processes. Addressed each separately per BSB's format.

questions.requirement: Describe the user permissions options available. Can custom groups with custom permissions be created? Are the groups pre-canned if so please list and describe them.
questions.bullet:
Custom permission groups can be created with any combination of granular permissions. Pre-built templates are also provided. PRE-BUILT ROLE TEMPLATES:
- Program Administrator: Full cross-FI access, platform configuration, user management
- Agent Bank Administrator: Full access within their FI tenant
- Credit Analyst: Application review, adjudication, credit line management
- Fraud Analyst: Fraud alerts, case management, blocking, transaction review
- Call Center Agent: Account lookup, servicing, payments, card replacement
- Branch Agent: Application intake, limited servicing
- Compliance Officer: Audit logs, regulatory reports, AML screening
- Reporting Analyst: Read-only report and dashboard access
- Marketing Manager: Campaign and rewards configuration
- Read-Only Viewer: Dashboard access, no action permissions - Templates are starting points — BSB can modify any template or build from scratch
- Individual permissions are toggleable (e.g., "view PAN," "approve credit increase," "override fraud flag")
- Groups can be cloned and adjusted for quick setup
questions.paragraph: BSB can create custom permission groups with any combination of individual permissions. The platform also provides pre-built role templates as starting points: Program Administrator, Agent Bank Administrator, Credit Analyst, Fraud Analyst, Call Center Agent, Branch Agent, Compliance Officer, Reporting Analyst, Marketing Manager, and Read-Only Viewer. Each template can be modified, or BSB can build a role from scratch by toggling individual permissions. Permissions are granular — "view PAN," "approve credit limit increase," "override fraud flag," "process payment," "generate report," "configure product parameter" are each separate toggles. Groups can be cloned and adjusted for quick setup of similar roles with minor differences.

questions.requirement: What are the systems password requirements? Describe how they can be changed. Describe how BSB can set it differently for adminitrative staff and customers
questions.bullet:
Password requirements are configurable through the Brim admin portal. BSB can set different policies for admin staff and cardholders. - Minimum length: Configurable, 14+ characters supported
- Complexity: Uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters — each independently required or optional
- History: Configurable depth (e.g., last 12 passwords cannot be reused)
- Expiration: Configurable per group (e.g., 90 days for staff, 180 days for customers, or no expiration)
- Lockout: After configurable failed attempts
- Changes: BSB admins update policies through the admin portal. Changes take effect immediately for new passwords; existing passwords follow the new policy at next reset
- Separate policy profiles for admin staff and cardholders
questions.paragraph: Password requirements are configurable through the admin portal. BSB sets minimum length (14+ characters supported), complexity rules (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters — each toggled independently), history depth, expiration period, and lockout thresholds. BSB can maintain separate policy profiles for administrative staff and cardholders. Staff passwords might require 14 characters, 90-day expiration, and 12-password history. Cardholder passwords might require 10 characters, 180-day expiration, and 6-password history. Policy changes take effect immediately for new passwords. Existing passwords comply at the user's next reset.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about password requirements and whether they differ for staff vs customers. Confirmed both.

questions.requirement: How long is a login session maintained before it logs the user out? Describe how BSB can set this differently for customers and internal staff.
questions.bullet:
Session timeout is configurable per user type. BSB sets different values for cardholders and staff. - Inactivity timeout: Configurable duration (e.g., 15 minutes for staff, 10 minutes for cardholder portal)
- Absolute session timeout: Maximum session duration regardless of activity (e.g., 8 hours for staff)
- BSB sets the timeout values through the admin portal
- Active sessions can be terminated manually by administrators
- Session expiry forces re-authentication — no silent re-login
- Timeout values can differ by role or portal (admin portal vs. cardholder portal vs. mobile)
questions.paragraph: Session timeout is configurable per user type. BSB can set different inactivity timeout values for cardholder sessions and internal staff sessions — for example, 10 minutes of inactivity for cardholders, 15 minutes for staff. An absolute session timeout can also be set to cap total session duration regardless of activity. BSB configures timeout values through the admin portal. Changes take effect for new sessions. Administrators can manually terminate active sessions if needed — for example, to force logout after a role change or suspected compromise. When a session expires, the user must re-authenticate. There is no silent background re-login.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about session timeout and whether it differs for customers vs staff. Confirmed configurable per type.

Lockout duration is also configurable: a temporary lockout (e.g., 15 minutes) or permanent lockout until an admin manually unlocks the account. Different settings can apply to customers and staff. Cardholder accounts might lock for 15 minutes with self-service unlock via email or SMS verification. Staff accounts might lock until an admin unlocks them in the portal. Every failed login attempt is logged with timestamp, IP address, account ID, and failure reason. Automated alerts can be configured so BSB's security team receives notification after a defined number of failures on a single account.

questions.requirement: Describe the encryption that is used to protect connections to servers. Also explain the authentication scheme used to ensure connections are authroized between clients and servers
questions.bullet:
All connections are encrypted with TLS 1.2+. Authentication uses OAuth 2.0 tokens, session-based auth with MFA, and SSO protocols. ENCRYPTION:
- Data in transit: TLS 1.2 or higher for all client-server, API, portal, and processor connections
- Data at rest: AES-256 encryption
- TSYS connections: TLS 1.2 with PGP encryption for file transfers
- Key management: PCI-DSS compliant with regular rotation AUTHENTICATION:
- API access: OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens with configurable expiry
- Portal access: Session-based authentication over HTTPS with MFA
- SSO: SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, JWT (HS256, upgradable to RSA, 15-minute token expiry)
- Service-to-service: Mutual TLS (mTLS) where supported
- All authentication events logged with timestamp, source IP, and outcome
questions.paragraph: All connections between clients and servers are encrypted with TLS 1.2 or higher. Data at rest uses AES-256 encryption. TSYS processor connections use TLS 1.2 with PGP encryption applied to file transfers. Key management follows PCI-DSS requirements with regular rotation schedules. For authentication, API access uses OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens with configurable expiry. Portal access uses session-based authentication over HTTPS with MFA enforced. SSO supports SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and JWT with HS256 signing (upgradable to RSA). JWT tokens expire after 15 minutes. Service-to-service communication uses mutual TLS where supported. Every authentication event — successful or failed — is logged with timestamp, source IP, and outcome.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about encryption and authentication. Covered both comprehensively with protocol specifics.
questions.pricing: Y - Included.
questions.strategic: From audited docs: TLS 1.2 for all connections to TSYS processor (PGP, VPN, SOAP XML, API & Batch). Triple-layered firewalls (Incapsula → Leaseweb → local server). Encryption at rest and in transit. HSM redundancy. 18 endpoint/network security methods documented including IDS, IPS, SIEM, 2FA, POLP.
questions.reg_enable: REGULATORY ENABLEMENT: Brim's security and encryption posture is validated through three independent certifications — PCI DSS v4.0.1 (payment data encryption and key management), SOC 2 Type 2 (Coalfire-audited operational security controls), and ISO 27001 (information security management system) — plus the AMX DD infosec section. No security breaches or privacy incidents have occurred in five years of operation. This triple-certified, independently validated security framework provides BSB with examiner-ready evidence of encryption and data protection compliance.

questions.bullet: Yes. Brim integrates with BSB's Jack Henry core banking system to pull customer information during the application process via real-time API calls. This includes account holder details, existing product relationships, deposit balances, and relationship tenure. Access controls are configurable by BSB — defining which fields are pulled, who can view them, and how they inform credit decisioning. The integration supports both pre-fill (reducing applicant data entry) and enrichment (adding core data to credit files).
questions.paragraph:
Brim integrates with core banking platforms to pull existing customer data into the credit card application. When a known customer applies, the system retrieves name, tax ID, address, phone number, deposit balances, and relationship data to pre-fill the application, cutting manual entry and errors.

Brim has completed core integrations with Fiserv (MANULIFE migrated from Fiserv), Temenos T24, i2c (Zolve/Continental Bank US deployment), and Collabria (UNI migrated 80,000 cards). For BSB, integration with Jack Henry SilverLake is scoped as part of implementation — the pattern is identical: RESTful API calls to pull customer data, secured in transit and at rest, using Brim's 300+ API framework.

Access to pre-filled data is governed by role-based permissions. A branch banker sees name, address, and relationship summary. A call center agent sees the same plus phone and communication preferences. Self-serve customers see only their own data. Underwriters see the full financial profile including deposit balances and lending history. Pre-fill also supports relationship recognition, so existing deposit or lending relationships factor into underwriting decisions — BSB can offer better terms to established customers.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to provide separate application pathways for new vs. existing customers. For existing customers, describe how you capture the holistic bank relationship in the application process.
questions.bullet: Brim provides separate application paths for new and existing customers. Existing customers get a streamlined flow with pre-filled data from their banking relationship, and their full relationship — deposits, loans, payment history — is factored into the underwriting decision.
questions.paragraph: Brim's application engine differentiates between new and existing customers at the start of the process. Existing customers are identified through core banking integration (by SSN, account number, or login credentials for authenticated sessions), and their application path is shorter because known data is pre-filled. Their banking relationship — deposit balances, direct deposit history, lending history, payment behavior — is pulled into the application record and made available to the decisioning engine. This means an existing BSB customer with a strong deposit relationship can receive more favorable underwriting treatment, as configured by BSB's credit policy. New customers go through the full application flow with manual data entry and standard identity verification. The system also flags existing customers who may have prior negative history (charged-off accounts, blacklists) so those are routed appropriately. BSB defines the rules for how relationship data influences credit decisions — Brim's engine executes them.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about new vs. existing pathways and holistic relationship capture. This is a key differentiator for community banks where relationship banking matters. Brim's core banking integration (documented in Tech Overview Section 5.1) enables the relationship data pull. The dual-pathway approach is standard for modern card platforms. Marked GREEN because existing customer recognition and relationship-based decisioning are core to Brim's value proposition for bank partners.

questions.bullet: Brim captures signatures electronically across all channels — online, mobile, in-branch, and phone. Disclosures are presented and acknowledged inline, with a full audit trail recording what was shown, when it was accepted, the IP address or location, and a timestamp. Brim's application platform currently processes thousands of applications monthly across its live programs including Affinity Credit Union (community banking), Manulife (wealth management), and Zolve (US newcomer market). The platform supports sub-3-second credit decisions, 95%+ OCR document extraction accuracy, and same-day application-to-card issuance for digital cards.
questions.paragraph: Signature capture in Brim's platform is electronic across every channel. In-branch applications capture signatures through the banker's workstation or a tablet. Phone applications use a consent workflow where disclosures are read or sent electronically, with verbal consent recorded and documented. Every signature event generates a complete audit trail that includes the signer's identity, the exact disclosure text presented, the timestamp, IP address (for digital channels), device information, and the method of signature. This audit record is stored with the application and can be retrieved for compliance reviews, disputes, or regulatory examinations. Disclosures are versioned, so the system records exactly which version of each disclosure was presented to the applicant at the time of signing.

questions.requirement: What is the E-signature process? What is your capability of having an e-sign integration? What audit capabilities does this offer?
questions.bullet: Brim integrates e-signature into the application flow so applicants sign disclosures and agreements digitally without leaving the application. The e-signature process captures a full audit trail — signer identity, timestamp, IP address, and the exact document version signed.
questions.paragraph: E-signature is built into Brim's application process. When an applicant reaches the disclosure and agreement stage, documents are presented inline and signed electronically as part of the application flow — no separate e-sign portal or redirect required. The e-signature integration supports ESIGN Act and UETA compliance requirements. Each signature event records the signer's identity, the timestamp, the IP address, the device used, and a hash of the document signed, creating a tamper-evident audit record. If multiple documents require signature (cardholder agreement, disclosure acknowledgment, consent forms), each is presented individually with its own signature capture. These records are stored with the application file and are retrievable for compliance, legal, or dispute resolution purposes. The audit trail is immutable — once captured, signature records cannot be modified or deleted.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks specifically about e-signature process, integration options, and audit capabilities. E-signature is legally required for online credit card applications in the U.S. under the ESIGN Act. Brim's digital application workflow inherently includes e-signature functionality. The Consumer Platform Overview confirms the digital application and signing capabilities. Marked GREEN because e-signature is a fundamental processing requirement.
