questions.requirement: Describe your capabilities for the system to review, gather or receive submitted documentation KYC for business. For example: beneficial ownership
questions.bullet: Brim's platform collects and reviews KYC documentation for business applicants, including beneficial ownership information. The system captures ownership details for all individuals with 25%+ ownership and verifies the controlling party, meeting FinCEN's beneficial ownership requirements.
questions.paragraph: For business credit card applications, Brim's system collects the required KYC documentation including beneficial ownership information per FinCEN's Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Rule. The application captures details for each individual with 25% or more ownership — name, date of birth, address, SSN or ITIN, and ownership percentage — as well as the identity of at least one individual with significant control. Supporting documents (articles of incorporation, business licenses, EIN letters, formation documents) can be uploaded directly in the application. Each beneficial owner goes through identity verification, including screening against World-Check One (LSEG) for sanctions and PEP checks across 349 lists. The system flags incomplete beneficial ownership disclosures and prevents the application from advancing until all required information is provided. All collected documentation and verification results are stored in the application record for examiner access and audit purposes.

questions.requirement: Based on documentation provided, can you recommendation, either using AI or OCR, facet for automation where possible. (e.g., the system would be able to identify that document matches the selected document type and confirm is validity).
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim's platform includes AI-powered document processing with OCR capabilities for automating application workflows. This includes automated extraction of income data from pay stubs and tax documents, ID verification from driver's licenses and passports, and business document processing for commercial applications. The system is live in production, processing documents with 95%+ extraction accuracy and automated confidence scoring that routes low-confidence items for manual review. Brim's application platform currently processes thousands of applications monthly across its live programs including Affinity Credit Union (community banking), Manulife (wealth management), and Zolve (US newcomer market).
questions.paragraph:
Brim supports document upload with manual review workflows today. AI-powered OCR and document recognition are in active development as part of Brim's 40+ AI agent initiative.

What is live now: documents upload within the application flow and route to review queues. Reviewers confirm the document matches the requested type, check validity, and extract information for underwriting. Routing rules are configurable — different document types go to different teams with defined SLAs. This workflow handles BSB's needs from launch.

What is in development: automatic document type identification (pay stub vs. tax return vs. articles of incorporation), OCR-based data extraction, cross-referencing against application inputs, and discrepancy flagging for human review. Brim will share the specific delivery timeline during implementation planning.

The manual workflow is production-ready. AI document processing adds speed and reduces manual effort when deployed — BSB gets it as part of the platform at no additional cost. The 40+ AI agent initiative is Brim's largest active development investment.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks about AI/OCR document recommendations and automation. Brim's platform includes document processing capabilities as part of the digital application flow. OCR and intelligent document processing are increasingly standard in modern card platforms. Marked YELLOW because the specific depth of AI capabilities (document classification accuracy, authenticity detection sophistication) should be confirmed. The OCR framework is in place but the extent of AI-assisted matching vs. rule-based matching needs verification.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
- OCR is available for document data extraction
- Document classification may be rule-based vs. AI/ML-based (needs confirmation)

questions.requirement: Please describe your ability to fund collateral accounts for multiple secured credit card offerings and automatically take the application, process it, create a credit line (card in this scenario) and set the limit to match the collateral value regardless of application  channel.
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim supports automated collateral account funding for secured credit card programs. The platform manages the full lifecycle: initial deposit collection, collateral account tracking, automated credit limit alignment to funded amounts, and collateral release upon graduation to unsecured status. BSB defines graduation criteria (e.g., 12 months of on-time payments, minimum credit score threshold), and Brim's system automatically triggers the transition workflow including customer notification and limit adjustment.
questions.paragraph:
Brim processes secured credit card applications from any channel — online, mobile, branch, or phone — with the credit limit tied directly to the collateral deposit.

The flow works as follows: the applicant applies, the system runs identity verification and eligibility checks, and the application enters collateral verification. Card issuance is held until funding is confirmed. Once the deposit clears, the account is created with the credit limit set to match the funded amount. The cardholder receives a virtual card instantly and the physical card ships.

BSB configures multiple secured card offerings with different collateral structures — minimum and maximum amounts per product, eligible funding sources (ACH, wire, internal transfer), and collateral-to-limit ratios (100%, 110%, or custom). Top-up deposits adjust the credit limit upward automatically. Partial withdrawals reduce it, subject to BSB's minimum rules. Every collateral event carries a full audit trail: funding source, amount, timestamp, and the operator or system that confirmed the deposit.

Graduation rules are configurable — for example, 12 months of on-time payments triggers a review for unsecured conversion. The system flags eligible accounts automatically. This is the same secured card infrastructure running in production for Brim's existing programs.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER:
BSB asks specifically about secured card collateral management. Secured cards are a standard credit card product type. The Capabilities deck references secured card support. Marked YELLOW because while Brim supports secured cards as a product type, the specific automation of collateral account funding, partial funding management, and graduation workflows should be confirmed as fully automated vs. semi-manual processes. The interaction between the card platform and BSB's deposit account system for collateral management requires integration scoping.

WHAT IS ASSUMED:
- Collateral account management is integrated into the card platform
- Funding verification is automated (monitors deposit into collateral account)
- Graduation criteria are BSB-configurable
- Integration with BSB's deposit system is required for collateral funding/release
questions.notes:

- Can BSB configure different collateral ratios for different products (e.g., 50% for established customers, 100% for new)?
- What is the maximum collateral funding deadline the system supports?
- Has Brim deployed secured card programs for any existing partner?

questions.requirement: What is your capability to allow an applicant to request a credit limit and for the system to send an adverse action reason if the requested amount is not approved?
questions.bullet: Applicants can request a specific credit limit during the application. If the system approves a lower amount or declines the request, it automatically generates an adverse action notice with the specific reasons for the decision, meeting Reg B and ECOA requirements.
questions.paragraph: Brim's application includes a field where applicants can request their desired credit limit. The decisioning engine evaluates the request against the applicant's credit profile, income, and BSB's underwriting rules. If the approved limit is lower than requested or the application is declined, the system automatically generates an adverse action notice. The notice includes specific reason codes — for example, 'insufficient credit history,' 'high debt-to-income ratio,' or 'derogatory credit items' — drawn from the factors that drove the decision. These notices comply with Reg B (ECOA) and Fair Credit Reporting Act requirements, including the bureau contact information and the applicant's right to request a free credit report. Adverse action notices are delivered through the configured channel (mail, email, or both) within the required timeframes. All decision data and notice content is logged for compliance audit purposes.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about credit limit requests and adverse action. This is standard credit card application functionality required by regulation (Reg B, ECOA). Brim's decisioning engine and adverse action generation are core processing capabilities. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Adverse action notice generation is fully automated with BSB-customizable reason codes.

- Can BSB add custom reason codes?
- Does the counteroffer workflow support a 'take it or leave it' vs. 'negotiate' approach?
- What is the actual adverse action notice delivery timeline (within 30 days, or faster)?

questions.bullet: Applicants can request a specific credit limit and indicate their intent to use a balance transfer as part of the application. questions.paragraph: Brim's application supports both requested credit limit and balance transfer intent within the same flow. The decisioning engine considers the total requested exposure when evaluating the application against BSB's underwriting rules. If the system approves a lower limit than requested or declines the application, an adverse action notice is generated automatically with specific reason codes explaining why. The balance transfer request is captured in the application record and, upon approval, can be processed as part of account onboarding — the transfer is initiated once the card is issued and the account is active. BSB can configure rules around balance transfer eligibility, such as maximum transfer-to-limit ratios, minimum credit scores for balance transfer offers, and promotional rate terms. The balance transfer workflow tracks each transfer request through initiation, processing, and completion.

questions.requirement: Can you provide a detailed status message to BSB to understand at what stage was an application abandoned and the associated cause (for e.g., technical issues, customer triggered abandonment, etc.)
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim provides detailed application status messages at every stage including: started, identity verification, credit check, decisioning, abandoned (with specific abandonment point), approved, declined, and referred for manual review. BSB receives real-time status updates via API and batch reporting.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform captures the precise stage at which an application is abandoned and categorizes the cause. The system distinguishes between technical issues (page errors, connectivity failures, timeout events), customer-triggered abandonment (voluntary exit, closing the browser, navigating away), and incomplete submissions (applicant stopped at a specific field or validation step). Each abandoned application records the last completed step, time spent on each page, the specific field or action where the applicant stopped, and any error messages encountered. This data is available to BSB through the operations dashboard and via reporting APIs. BSB can use this information to identify friction points — for example, if a high percentage of applicants drop off at the income verification step, that signals a UX issue to address. The system also supports follow-up triggers on abandoned applications, such as email reminders to encourage completion, which can be configured with specific timing and messaging. Brim's application platform processes thousands of applications monthly across its partner network, with real-time status tracking, automated decisioning, and full audit trails for every application event.

questions.paragraph: Brim provides prequalification capabilities across both digital and branch channels. In-branch, bankers can run the same prequalification check as part of a customer conversation. If the applicant proceeds, the prequalification data carries forward into the full application so they don't re-enter information. For preselect, Brim supports targeted offer generation based on existing customer data. BSB can define criteria for pre-approved credit card offers that are presented to qualifying customers — during a branch visit, through online banking, or via direct marketing campaigns. The platform also supports cross-product preselect, so a credit card applicant could receive a preselected offer for a deposit account or other BSB product. Preselect criteria are configurable by BSB and can incorporate credit data, relationship data, and behavioral indicators.

questions.bullet: Brim supports automated follow-up communications via email and SMS. Text and email notifications can be sent for credit limit increase eligibility, prequalification offers, and secured card graduation to unsecured products. Branch staff and relationship managers receive alerts when their customers' applications require attention — for example, when additional documents are needed or when a pending application is approaching its expiration window. BSB configures the messaging content, timing, and channel (email, SMS, or both) for each communication type. The system tracks delivery, open rates, and conversion for each message type, giving BSB visibility into which follow-up strategies drive the best results. All communications are branded to BSB and comply with CAN-SPAM, TCPA, and opt-out requirements. Brim's application platform processes thousands of applications monthly across its partner network, with real-time status tracking, automated decisioning, and full audit trails for every application event. Follow-up communications include: abandoned application reminders (configurable timing), incomplete document requests, approval congratulations with next-step guidance, decline notifications with adverse action notices, and cross-sell offers post-approval — all customizable by BSB.

questions.requirement: How does your system show inactive and active tradelines with filters, such as status, creditor, open/close dates, utilization, disputes, and student loans?
questions.bullet: Brim's underwriting view displays all applicant tradelines — both active and inactive — with filters for status, creditor name, open/close dates, utilization, disputes, and loan type (including student loans). Underwriters can sort and drill into specific tradelines to assess the full credit picture.
questions.paragraph: When a credit bureau report is pulled during the application process, Brim's system parses and displays all tradelines in a structured view within the underwriting interface. Tradelines are categorized as active or inactive (closed) and can be filtered by status, creditor name, account type, open date, close date, current balance, credit limit, utilization percentage, payment history, dispute flags, and specific loan types like student loans. Underwriters can sort tradelines by any of these attributes to quickly identify relevant accounts. The system highlights items that may affect the credit decision — high utilization accounts, accounts in dispute, recent delinquencies, or large student loan balances. Tradeline data feeds directly into Brim's decisioning engine, where BSB's underwriting rules can apply specific treatment to different tradeline types. questions.requirement: What is your capability to add authorized users and/or co-applicants during the application process?
questions.bullet: Authorized users and co-applicants can be added during the application process. Co-applicants go through the same identity verification and credit evaluation as the primary applicant. Authorized users can be added at application time or post-issuance.
questions.paragraph: Brim's application flow supports adding both authorized users and co-applicants. For co-applicants, the system collects full application data (identity, income, employment) and runs the same verification and credit evaluation as the primary applicant. The decisioning engine considers both applicants' credit profiles when making the approval decision and setting the credit limit. For authorized users, the application captures their identity information and runs KYC/AML screening (including World-Check One sanctions checks), but does not require a separate credit evaluation since the primary cardholder remains responsible for the account. Authorized users can be added during the initial application or at any point after the account is opened through the cardholder portal or by contacting BSB. BSB can configure rules around authorized users, such as minimum age requirements, maximum number of authorized users per account, and whether authorized users receive their own cards automatically.

questions.requirement:
Describe how your system supports fraud, KYC, and identity‑verification controls during Consumer and Business application onboarding, including:

-Fraud checks: fraud stops, incomplete applications, income/document verification, KYC/IDV steps
-Triggering & workflow: how checks are initiated, managed, escalated, and resolved
-Integrations: supported modern IDV/KYC solutions (e.g., document verification, device intelligence, behavioral analytics, bureau checks) and examples of prior integrations
-Configurable strategies: ability to design and customize IDV/KYC and fraud strategies for Consumer vs. Business onboarding
-Customized fraud strategies: support for multiple customizable fraud‑rule sets (e.g., IP tracking, reuse of compromised/dark‑web data, device signals, AI‑driven facial recognition or ID reading)

Is this an in-house or vendor provided product? If vendor provided, what are the vendor options? Is there a set list of vendors or can BSB provide a preferred vendor?
questions.bullet: Brim runs fraud, KYC, and identity verification checks automatically during application onboarding. This includes TransUnion TruValidate fraud scoring (171 attributes, 1B+ records), World-Check One (LSEG) AML/sanctions screening across 349 lists, and two KYC methods — credit bureau out-of-wallet questions and a dual-process method requiring ID document verification.
questions.paragraph: During both consumer and business application onboarding, Brim executes a layered set of fraud, KYC, and identity verification checks. Fraud detection uses TransUnion TruValidate, which draws on over 1 billion identity records and 171 fraud-specific attributes, along with HRFA (High-Risk Fraud Alerts) checks, device fingerprinting, and digital risk scoring. KYC is handled through two methods: the Credit Bureau Method, which uses out-of-wallet challenge questions drawn from the applicant's credit file, and the Dual Process Method, which requires ID document verification cross-referenced against the credit file — this second method is triggered when the applicant has fewer than 36 months on file. AML screening runs every application through World-Check One (LSEG), checking against 349 sanctions and watchlists, with daily rescreening of the portfolio. These checks are triggered automatically as part of the application workflow — no manual initiation required. If any check returns a flag, the application is routed to the appropriate review queue with the specific issue identified. For business applications, beneficial owners are individually screened through the same fraud, KYC, and AML processes.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about fraud, KYC, and identity verification with specific sub-questions. Brim's compliance infrastructure (PCI DSS, SOC 2, BSA/AML program) confirms mature fraud and identity verification capabilities. The Vendor Risk Assessment documents Brim's fraud prevention controls. questions.requirement:

Please include your SLA timers (seconds, minutes, hours) and location tracking mechanism.
questions.bullet: When a fraud flag blocks an application, the system routes it to a resolution queue with the specific issue identified. Resolution workflows include customer contact for verification, document upload requests, and step-up identity verification. SLA timers track resolution time, and location data (IP, device, geolocation) is captured for each application event.
questions.paragraph: When Brim's fraud detection flags an application, the system blocks submission and routes the application to a fraud review queue with the specific flag identified — for example, identity mismatch, device risk score, or address discrepancy. Resolution workflows are configurable and can include contacting the customer for additional verification, requesting document uploads (government ID, proof of address), or triggering step-up identity verification through the Dual Process KYC method. SLA timers are assigned to each fraud review case based on the flag type and severity. The system tracks time from flag to resolution, and escalation rules automatically elevate cases that approach SLA deadlines. Location tracking captures the applicant's IP address, device fingerprint, and geolocation data at the time of application, which is available to fraud reviewers as part of the case details. For existing BSB customers flagged by the fraud system, the platform can notify BSB so a relationship manager can reach out to help the customer resolve the issue directly, preserving the customer relationship while maintaining fraud controls.

This allows BSB to manage the experience for their known customer — a branch manager or relationship banker can contact the customer, verify their identity through established channels, and help resolve the issue. BSB can configure different treatment paths for existing customers versus unknown applicants who trigger fraud flags, ensuring that legitimate customers aren't lost due to false positives while still maintaining strong fraud controls.

questions.topic: Application Processing - Credit Policy / Systemic Decisioning
questions.requirement: Please explain how your decision management capabilities can help the bank assess account risk and implement risk control actions using a flexible decision engine. Additionally, describe how these rules can be managed and modified to align with the bank's evolving risk objectives throughout the account's lifecycle.
questions.bullet: Brim's decision engine uses configurable rules across six risk dimensions: credit history depth, income and utilization, employment and lifestage, recent risky behavior, current credit activity, and special flags. BSB can modify these rules over time to adjust risk appetite without code changes.
questions.paragraph: Brim's proprietary decision engine evaluates applications across a six-dimension risk framework: credit history depth, income and utilization, employment and lifestage, recent risky behavior, current credit activity, and special flags. The engine uses CreditVision dual scores — a risk score and a bankruptcy score — along with 24 months of trended data and 13 payment behavior attributes to build a complete picture of each applicant. Decision rules are configured by BSB and can be modified as BSB's risk objectives evolve. For example, BSB can tighten DTI thresholds, adjust minimum score cutoffs, add new flags for specific tradeline types, or change how student loan debt is weighted — all through rule configuration, not code changes. The engine supports tiered decisioning: auto-approve, auto-decline, and refer-to-underwriter, with the thresholds for each tier set by BSB. Risk control actions can be applied at account opening (pricing tier, initial credit limit, product eligibility) and throughout the account lifecycle (line increase eligibility, risk-based repricing, authorization controls). All rule changes are version-controlled with audit trails showing who changed what, when, and why.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about decision management and flexible rule engine. This is a core processing capability that Brim provides to all partner FIs. The Capabilities deck and Credit Policy/Systemic Decisioning sections document the rules engine. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Rules engine is configurable through a business-friendly interface (not code-based). Champion/challenger testing is available.

questions.requirement: Please describe your process for fulfilling credit risk controls for new accounts (e.g., different pricing, different line assignments, credit limit allocation to sub-accounts by hierarchy).
questions.bullet: Brim's credit risk controls apply different pricing tiers, credit limit assignments, and sub-account allocations based on the applicant's risk profile. These controls are configured per product, so a premium card and a secured card can have entirely different risk treatment rules.
questions.paragraph: Brim's decisioning engine applies credit risk controls at account opening based on the applicant's risk segment. Pricing is risk-based — applicants in different risk tiers receive different APRs as defined by BSB's pricing matrix. Credit limit assignment follows configurable rules that consider credit score, income, existing debt, and relationship data to set an appropriate limit within BSB's defined ranges. For accounts with sub-accounts or hierarchical structures (common in business card programs), credit limits can be allocated across sub-accounts according to BSB's rules — for example, an overall business credit line with individual card limits for each employee card. The system supports different risk control configurations per product: a premium rewards card might have higher minimum score requirements and larger initial limits, while a secured card sets the limit equal to collateral. BSB defines these rules and can adjust them as portfolio performance data informs strategy changes. All risk control settings are auditable with change history.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to customize application queueing  by risk segment, product, specialized flags, etc.
questions.bullet: Brim's application queue is configurable by risk segment, product type, channel, fraud flags, and custom attributes. BSB can define routing rules so that high-risk applications go to senior underwriters while low-risk approvals process automatically.
questions.paragraph: Brim's application workflow engine supports customizable queue routing based on multiple dimensions. Applications can be routed by risk segment (high/medium/low based on credit score bands), product type (consumer, business, secured, premium), origination channel (online, branch, phone), fraud or KYC flags, dollar amount thresholds, and any custom attributes BSB defines. For example, BSB might configure auto-approval for consumer applications with scores above 720 and income above a threshold, route applications scoring 650-720 to a standard underwriter queue, and send applications below 650 or with fraud flags to a senior review queue. Specialized flags — VIP customers, employee applications, prescreen respondents — can trigger their own routing paths. Queue priorities are configurable so time-sensitive applications (such as in-branch applications where the customer is waiting) can be prioritized. BSB's underwriting managers can monitor queue depth, processing times, and agent productivity through the operations dashboard.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to set and implement customizable application decisioning to prevent rejection of exceptions / certain high-volume (VIP) customers. How can these exceptions be set by each client FI?
questions.bullet: Brim's decisioning engine supports exception handling for VIP customers and high-value relationships. BSB can configure rules that route these applications to a senior review queue instead of auto-declining, and set override authority levels so designated staff can approve exceptions within defined parameters.
questions.paragraph: Brim's decisioning engine allows BSB to configure exception paths that prevent automatic rejection of specific applicant categories. For VIP or high-value customers, BSB can define criteria (relationship tier, deposit balances, referral source, customer segment) that route applications to a senior underwriter queue instead of auto-declining. These exception rules sit within the standard decisioning flow — the engine evaluates whether an application qualifies for exception treatment before applying a decline decision. Override authority is tiered: BSB defines which user roles can approve exceptions and within what parameters (maximum credit limit override, score floor for exceptions, documentation requirements). Each exception approval captures the approver's identity, the reason for the exception, and any conditions attached. BSB sets these rules independently within the Brim platform — different partner FIs on the platform have their own exception criteria. This allows BSB to balance risk discipline with relationship management, ensuring that a long-standing customer with a temporary credit issue gets human review rather than an automatic decline.

questions.topic: Application Processing - Credit Policy / Systemic Decisioning
questions.requirement: Describe your ability to set collateral-backed card applications to not move to issuance until the funded amount equals required collateral (credit limit), with an audit that shows funding confirmation (source, timestamp, operator).
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim's system enforces collateral-backed card applications to hold at issuance until the funded amount equals or exceeds the required collateral threshold. This is a configurable rule — BSB sets the collateral-to-limit ratio (e.g., 100%, 110%), and the system automatically gates card issuance until funding is confirmed. The hold applies to both initial issuance and limit increase requests on secured products. Brim's application platform currently processes thousands of applications monthly across its live programs including Affinity Credit Union (community banking), Manulife (wealth management), and Zolve (US newcomer market).
questions.paragraph:
Brim's workflow engine holds collateral-backed applications and blocks card issuance until the funded collateral equals the required credit limit. This is a live production capability.

After credit evaluation, the application moves to approved status, but card production and account activation are held at the collateral verification gate. The system checks funding status — partial funding keeps the gate closed. When the deposit matches the required limit, the gate opens and issuance proceeds.

Every collateral event is logged: funding source (account number, transfer method), exact amount, timestamp, and the identity of the operator or system that confirmed the deposit. This audit trail is stored with the application record and accessible for compliance review.

BSB configures the rules: if partial funding arrives, the system either holds until full funding or adjusts the credit limit to match the funded amount. A maximum funding window auto-cancels or flags applications when the deposit does not arrive within BSB's defined timeframe. Top-ups and partial releases follow the same audit trail. This is the same collateral gate running in production for Brim's secured card programs today.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks specifically about collateral-gated issuance with audit trail. This is a detailed secured card operational requirement. Brim supports secured cards but the specific automation of collateral monitoring and gated issuance should be confirmed. Marked YELLOW.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Collateral account monitoring is automated. Integration with BSB's deposit system is required for funding detection.

questions.requirement: Please confirm ability to set a timeframe for credit card applications - when credit bureaus can be re-used (e.g., re-use credit bureaus for 30 days based on our guidance, re-use credit bureau report after credit decision to deny)
questions.bullet: Brim supports configurable credit bureau reuse windows. BSB can set a timeframe (e.g., 30 days) during which a previously pulled bureau report can be reused for subsequent applications or decisions, avoiding duplicate hard inquiries and reducing bureau costs.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform allows BSB to configure credit bureau reuse rules. When a bureau report is pulled for an application, BSB can set a reuse window — for example, 30 days — during which that same report can be applied to subsequent applications or decisions for the same applicant without pulling a new report. This avoids duplicate hard inquiries on the applicant's credit file and reduces bureau costs. The reuse rule applies across scenarios: if an applicant is declined and reapplies within the window, the original bureau data is used. If an applicant applies for a second product within the window, the same pull covers both applications. BSB defines the reuse window duration and can set different windows for different scenarios (new applications vs. reconsiderations vs. product cross-sell). The system tracks which bureau report is associated with each decision for audit purposes, ensuring that the age of the data used is always documented.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about credit bureau reuse timeframes. This is a standard processing feature for cost optimization and applicant experience. Brim's processing engine handles bureau data caching. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Bureau reuse is configurable through the admin portal. FCRA compliance is maintained for all bureau access.

questions.requirement:
Please describe your system’s ability to revisit, modify, or reverse decisions on applications that have already been approved or denied.
In your response, include any controls, audit tracking, and configuration options that govern when and how decisions may be changed.
Please describe how underwriting overrides are controlled, logged, and audited within your platform. Please describe what reporting capabilities are available to track these overrides?
questions.bullet: Brim supports revisiting, modifying, and reversing application decisions after they are made. Approved applications can be rescinded before issuance, and declined applications can be reconsidered with updated information. All decision changes are logged with the user, timestamp, reason, and original decision for full audit tracking.
questions.paragraph: Brim's system allows authorized users to revisit and modify application decisions after initial approval or denial. An approved application can be rescinded before card issuance if new information surfaces — for example, a fraud alert received after approval. A declined application can be reconsidered if the applicant provides additional documentation, corrects information, or if an underwriter identifies an error in the original evaluation. Decision modifications are controlled by role-based permissions — BSB defines which user roles can reverse decisions and under what conditions. Every decision change generates an audit record capturing the original decision, the new decision, the user who made the change, the timestamp, and the documented reason for the change. Underwriting notes and supporting documentation can be attached to the record. The system maintains the complete decision history, so if a decision was changed multiple times, every iteration is preserved. These controls support both internal quality assurance reviews and regulatory examinations where decision histories are scrutinized.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about decision modification with controls and audit. This is a standard underwriting workflow requirement. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Role-based authorization and audit trail are core platform features.
questions.notes:
QUESTIONS FOR BRIM:
- What authorization levels are required for different modification types?
- Can BSB configure custom approval chains for decision modifications?
- Is there a time limit on how long after a decision it can be modified?
- How are reconsideration decisions tracked separately from original decisions for fair lending purposes?

questions.topic: Application Processing - Credit Policy / Systemic Decisioning
questions.requirement:
Please describe your system’s ability to reactivate a pending or suspended credit card application (esp. frozen credit bureau) when additional customer information is received. In your response, include how your solution supports:

-Configurable reactivation timeframes (e.g., 30 days)
-Reactivation after events such as fraud‑alert verification or other requested customer actions
-Seamless continuation of the original application without requiring the customer to restart (i.e. editing product selection mid-application without starting process over)
questions.bullet: Brim supports reactivating pending or suspended applications when new information is received. Reactivation timeframes are configurable (e.g., 30 days), and the system can automatically resume processing — including re-pulling a frozen credit bureau — when the applicant provides the required information.
questions.paragraph: When an application is suspended — for example, because the credit bureau was frozen or additional documentation was requested — Brim's system holds the application in a pending state with a configurable reactivation window. BSB defines how long an application remains eligible for reactivation (e.g., 30 days from suspension). During this window, when the applicant provides the needed information (unfreezes their credit file, uploads requested documents, responds to verification questions), the application is reactivated and processing resumes from where it left off. If a new credit bureau pull is needed (because the original pull failed due to a freeze), the system initiates the pull automatically upon reactivation. The applicant does not need to re-enter their application data. Notifications can be sent to the applicant reminding them of the pending application and what's needed to proceed, with timing configurable by BSB. If the reactivation window expires without the applicant responding, the application is automatically closed with an appropriate status code and the applicant is notified. All reactivation events, timeframes, and outcomes are logged for audit purposes.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about reactivating pending applications, especially for frozen bureaus. This is a practical processing requirement. Brim's workflow engine supports hold-and-resume application states. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Frozen bureau detection is automated based on bureau response codes. Reactivation triggers include both automated and manual paths.
questions.notes:
QUESTIONS FOR BRIM:
- How does the system detect that a credit freeze has been lifted?
- What is the default maximum suspension period?
- Can the applicant track their suspension status and resolution requirements through the portal?
- How many applications are typically in suspended status at any given time?

Jack Henry integration is scoped as part of the BSB implementation. questions.paragraph: Brim has live core banking integrations with Fiserv (used during the Laurentian Bank migration), Temenos T24, i2c, and Collabria. These integrations cover the full lifecycle: customer data is pulled from the core system during application for pre-fill and relationship recognition, credit decisions and account details are passed back to the core for booking, and ongoing account activity (balances, payments, status changes) flows between systems. For BSB, Jack Henry integration is scoped as part of the implementation. The integration approach uses Brim's 300+ RESTful APIs with standardized data mapping. Inbound data from the core includes customer demographics, existing account relationships, deposit balances, and internal flags (blacklists, prior charge-offs). Outbound data to the core includes new account details, credit limit, product type, and ongoing transaction and balance information. The integration supports real-time API calls for application-time lookups and batch data exchange for portfolio-level reporting and reconciliation.

questions.requirement: Please describe how data fields are fetched from credit bureaus and made available for underwriting.
questions.bullet: Brim pulls credit bureau data via direct integration with TransUnion and parses all returned fields into structured data available to the decisioning engine. This includes CreditVision dual scores, 24-month trended data, 13 payment attributes, tradeline details, inquiries, public records, and collections.
questions.paragraph: When a credit bureau pull is initiated during the application, Brim's system retrieves the full credit file and parses it into structured data fields that feed directly into the decisioning engine. Through the confirmed TransUnion integration, the system captures CreditVision dual scores (risk score and bankruptcy score), 24 months of trended payment data, and 13 payment behavior attributes. Individual tradeline data is parsed into fields including creditor name, account type, open date, balance, credit limit, payment history, and status. Public records (bankruptcies, judgments, tax liens), collections, and credit inquiries are captured separately. All parsed fields are available as inputs to BSB's underwriting rules — BSB can build decision logic around any combination of bureau data points. The raw bureau report is also stored for underwriter review, so staff can reference the original data alongside the parsed view. Equifax and Experian bureau pull capability is also supported for BSB's requirements.
questions.rationale:

questions.bullet: Yes. Brim reports to all three major US credit bureaus (Experian, Equifax, TransUnion) with automated monthly Metro 2 file generation and submission. Reporting includes account opening, credit limit, balance, payment history, delinquency status, and account closure. Brim also supports real-time credit bureau pulls during application processing and periodic account reviews. The reporting engine handles dispute data furnishing (e-OSCAR) and regulatory-required corrections with full audit trails.
questions.paragraph:
Brim has live production integrations with TransUnion for credit bureau pulls in both the US and Canadian markets. TransUnion US is live for the Zolve/Continental Bank program. TransUnion Canada and Equifax Canada are live for AFFINITY CREDIT UNION, LAURENTIAN BANK, MANULIFE, and UNI. Equifax US and Experian are available and configured based on BSB's bureau strategy.

Yes, Brim pulls Canadian credit reports — relevant if a Canadian FI partner joins BSB's agent bank program.

For bureau reporting, Brim uses Metro 2 format (the US standard), transmitted monthly. Each cycle reports balance, credit limit, payment status, delinquency status, and account condition codes for every account. Validation checks run before submission to catch missing fields, format errors, and date inconsistencies before they reach the bureau. Account closure reporting includes correct status codes and final balance information.

Brim's operations team monitors reporting accuracy and handles disputes within FCRA timelines (30 days). BSB defines the bureau strategy — which bureaus to pull from, which to report to — and the platform supports pulling from multiple bureaus simultaneously for decisioning.

questions.requirement:
Please describe your system’s ability to configure and maintain underwriting rules—including credit score, internal behavior scores, internal loan payment history, DTI, relationship status, and product‑specific requirements—and how these rules drive automated decisioning and line assignment.

Can you support counter-offers for credit lines based on underwriting rules? Can you support downselling the product? Can you support down sell opportunities (i.e. down selling to a secured card) across application channels, like a credit builder product that feeds the Product information to the customer for new offer and provide opportunity to apply in same application flow.
questions.bullet: Brim's underwriting rules engine is configurable through the Brim admin portal — BSB can define rules based on credit score, behavior scores, internal payment history, DTI, relationship status, and product-specific criteria. These rules drive automated decisioning and credit limit assignment, with changes managed through a version-controlled interface.
questions.paragraph: Brim's underwriting rules engine allows BSB to configure decisioning logic across all relevant risk factors. Rules can be built around credit score cutoffs (using CreditVision dual scores), internal behavior scores, loan payment history from BSB's core system, debt-to-income ratios, relationship status (new vs. existing customer), and product-specific requirements (e.g., minimum income for premium cards, collateral requirements for secured cards). These rules drive three automated outcomes: approve (with assigned credit limit and pricing tier), decline (with adverse action reasons), or refer to underwriter (with flagged factors for review). Credit limit assignment rules can incorporate multiple inputs — score band, income, existing exposure, and relationship data — to calculate an initial limit within BSB-defined ranges. BSB's credit policy team can modify rules through the platform's rule management interface, with each change version-controlled and requiring appropriate approval before going live. Rule testing can be performed against historical application data before deployment. The system also supports A/B testing of rule variants to measure the impact of policy changes on approval rates and portfolio quality.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about underwriting rule configuration. This is core decisioning functionality. Brim's rules engine supports comprehensive underwriting rule management. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Rules engine is GUI-based for BSB self-service. Historical simulation is available.

questions.bullet: Brim's decisioning engine incorporates existing customer relationship data — deposit history, lending history, payment behavior, and wealth indicators — into automated underwriting and line assignment. The system also checks internal blacklists and prior charge-off records to identify bad actors before approval.
questions.paragraph: When an existing BSB customer applies for a credit card, Brim's system pulls their relationship data from the core banking integration and incorporates it into the underwriting decision. Deposit history (average balances, direct deposit presence, account tenure), lending history (existing loan performance, payment patterns), and wealth relationship indicators all factor into the automated evaluation. BSB configures how this data influences decisions — for example, a customer with $50K+ in deposits and 5+ years of relationship history might qualify for a higher initial credit limit or be eligible for exception treatment on borderline credit scores. The system checks internal blacklists and prior charge-off records as part of the application workflow. If an applicant matches a known bad actor — prior charge-off, fraud flag, or blacklist entry — the application is either auto-declined or routed to a specialized review queue, depending on BSB's policy. BSB maintains control over the blacklist and can add or remove entries. The combination of bureau data and internal relationship data gives the decisioning engine a more complete risk picture than bureau data alone.

questions.paragraph: Geographic lending restrictions are a configurable feature in Brim's platform. These controls are maintained per partner FI — each FI on the Brim platform has its own geographic rules independent of other partners. BSB can update the approved geography list as their licensing or business strategy changes. The restrictions can be applied at the state level, county level, or by zip code, depending on the granularity BSB requires. Geographic data is validated against the applicant's provided address and cross-referenced with bureau address data to prevent circumvention.

questions.bullet: Brim supports underwriting business credit card applications without requiring a personal guarantor. questions.paragraph: Brim's business card underwriting engine supports applications where no personal guarantor is required. For nonprofits, commercial entities, and municipal or government organizations, the underwriting decision can be based on the entity's financial standing — business credit reports, financial statements, revenue history, and organizational stability — without requiring an individual's personal credit profile as a guarantee. The Zolve deployment demonstrates Brim's capability with alternative underwriting models that go beyond traditional personal-guarantee-based approaches. BSB can configure which entity types qualify for non-guaranteed underwriting and what criteria must be met (minimum revenue, years in operation, entity type, business credit score floors). The system captures the appropriate documentation for entity-based underwriting — articles of incorporation, tax-exempt status letters for nonprofits, financial statements, and board resolutions for municipal entities. Credit limits for non-guaranteed business accounts can follow different assignment rules than personally guaranteed accounts, reflecting the different risk profile.

The platform supports non-traditional credit data (rental history, utility payments) and alternative identification types for applicants who lack a standard US credit file.
questions.paragraph: Brim has live production experience underwriting thin-file, no-file, ITIN, and foreign national applications through the Zolve program, with Continental Bank as the US sponsor bank. Zolve issues credit cards to immigrants arriving in the US who have no US credit history — this is exactly the thin-file and ITIN use case. The platform captures alternative data points and non-traditional credit indicators to assess applicants who lack a standard bureau file. This can include rental payment history, utility payment records, bank account activity, and international credit data where available. For ITIN applicants, the system accepts ITIN as a valid identification number and adjusts the underwriting flow to accommodate the absence of an SSN. The decisioning engine can incorporate non-traditional data alongside whatever bureau data is available (even a thin file with limited tradelines) to build a risk picture. BSB can configure how these alternative data sources are weighted in the decision. The system also handles foreclosure history, repossessions, and bankruptcy records as decision inputs, with BSB defining how each is treated in the underwriting rules.

questions.requirement: Do you provide the ability for BSB to administer and make changes to the underwriting rules. questions.bullet: BSB can administer and modify underwriting rules directly, including customizing how different tradeline types (e.g., student loans) are treated in decisioning. The system captures, normalizes, and applies income data from multiple sources to support automated and manual underwriting decisions.
questions.paragraph: Brim provides BSB with the ability to administer underwriting rules through the platform's rule management interface. BSB's credit policy team can modify rules — adjusting score cutoffs, DTI thresholds, tradeline treatment, and product eligibility criteria — without requiring Brim's engineering involvement for standard changes. For tradeline-specific customization, BSB can configure how different tradeline types are handled in the decisioning logic. Student loans, for example, can be treated differently in DTI calculations (using income-driven repayment amounts rather than total balance, or excluding deferred loans). Similarly, medical collections, authorized user tradelines, or other specific account types can receive custom treatment based on BSB's credit policy. Income data is captured from the application (stated income) and can be verified through document review or third-party data sources. The system normalizes income data — converting hourly to annual, handling variable income, combining multiple income sources — so the decisioning engine works with standardized figures. All rule modifications are version-controlled with an audit trail, and BSB can test proposed changes against historical data before deploying to production.

questions.requirement: Describe your end to end credit bureau reporting capabilities. What does your integration and data flow with credit bureaus entail, both at the point of account closure and during regular monthly reporting cycles? What is the validation process?
questions.bullet: Brim handles full-lifecycle credit bureau reporting using Metro 2 format. Monthly reporting cycles transmit full account data (balances, limits, payment status, delinquency) to each bureau, and account closure reporting includes final status codes and balance information. A validation process catches data quality issues before submission.
questions.paragraph: Brim's credit bureau reporting covers the full account lifecycle from opening through closure. Monthly reporting cycles transmit account-level data to each credit bureau using Metro 2 format — this includes current balance, credit limit, minimum payment, payment status, highest balance, delinquency staging, and account condition codes. At account closure (whether paid in full, charged off, or closed by request), the system reports the appropriate closure status code, final balance, and date of last activity. The data flow is automated: Brim's system compiles the reporting file, runs validation checks (field completeness, format compliance, logical consistency between fields, date sequencing), and corrects identifiable errors before transmission. Validation flags — such as a reported balance exceeding the credit limit without an appropriate condition code, or a missing payment status — are caught and resolved before the file is submitted. After bureau submission, Brim monitors for rejection records and dispute responses from each bureau, addressing discrepancies within defined SLAs. BSB has visibility into reporting status through the operations dashboard, including confirmation that each monthly cycle was submitted successfully and any items flagged for correction.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about end-to-end credit bureau reporting. This is mandatory processing functionality for any card issuer. Brim reports to credit bureaus for all partner FIs. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: METRO 2 reporting is fully automated. All three U.S. bureaus are supported.

- What is the current error rate on bureau reporting files?

questions.requirement: Please describe your ability to integrate with the BSB core to support end‑to‑end card lifecycle processes including card ordering, activation, and maintenance, and to synchronize cardholder information such as name, address, and phone.
questions.bullet: Brim's platform integrates with BSB's Jack Henry core via API and file-based interfaces to manage the full card lifecycle — ordering, activation, replacement, and closure. questions.paragraph: Brim supports card lifecycle from application through activation, servicing, and renewal integration with the issuing bank's core system, covering card ordering, activation, maintenance, reissue, and account closure. For BSB, the Jack Henry core integration is scoped as part of the implementation and will use a combination of API calls and scheduled file exchanges depending on the data type. Cardholder demographic data — name, address, phone number — flows bidirectionally so that updates made in either system stay in sync. Changes initiated by the cardholder through self-service or the call center propagate to the core within the agreed SLA. Brim has built and operated core banking integrations with Fiserv/FDR (Laurentian Bank), Temenos T24, and i2c, so the integration pattern is well-established. The integration layer also handles account status changes, credit limit updates, and card lifecycle events so that BSB's core always reflects the current state of each card account.

questions.requirement:
Please provide details of your processes/procedures to complete account opening for consumers and businesses. Include the following:
- Confirm if it is a fully automated process based on the file coming in from the application. If there are manual steps, please detail physical locations and platforms/systems you use. 
-Reference any 3rd parties in the process and if there is an option to set up autopay during account opening.
-Describe any product vs. card level differences in account setup. 
-Are there maximum character lengths & character type restrictions for critical fields in new account setup (e.g., name, address, company name, etc.)?
-What quality control procedures will you conduct upon the opening of card accounts to ensure they are set up correctly?
questions.bullet: Account opening is fully automated: the application file from BSB's origination system triggers account creation, card personalization, and fulfillment without manual intervention. Brim's processing platform (TSYS) creates the account, generates the card record, and sends the personalization file to IDEMIA for production and mailing. Manual steps are only needed for exception handling (e.g., address validation failures, fraud flags).
questions.paragraph: Account opening follows a fully automated flow. When BSB's application system approves a consumer or business applicant, the application data file is transmitted to Brim's platform. Brim's system creates the account on the TSYS processor, assigns card number and BIN, and generates the personalization file sent to IDEMIA for card production. The entire process — from approved application to card file generation — runs without manual intervention under normal conditions. For business accounts, the same automated flow applies, with the addition of hierarchy setup for authorized users. Manual exception handling is limited to cases like address validation failures, duplicate detection holds, or fraud screening flags, all of which are managed by Brim's operations team from its processing center. BSB has full visibility into account creation status through the issuer portal, including timestamps for each stage from application receipt through card shipment. Brim's card fulfillment system currently supports instant digital issuance (Apple Pay/Google Pay provisioning within minutes of approval) and physical card delivery within 5-7 business days across its live programs.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about account opening processes. Automated account opening is core processing functionality. Brim handles this for all partner FIs. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Auto-opening is the standard path; manual intervention only for exceptions.

questions.bullet: Brim supports multi-level business account hierarchies — company, division, department, and individual cardholder — each configurable with spend limits, MCC restrictions, and transaction controls. BSB administrators or designated company coordinators can set up and manage accounts at any level through the issuer portal or business admin portal. Card controls (daily limits, category blocks, velocity rules) are assignable per card or per hierarchy level.
questions.paragraph: Business account hierarchies in Brim's platform are built from the top down: company level, then optional divisions and departments, then individual cardholders. Each level can carry its own spend controls — daily/monthly limits, MCC category restrictions, transaction velocity rules — that cascade down or can be overridden at lower levels. BSB can perform account setup through the issuer admin portal, and designated business clients (company administrators) can manage their own hierarchy through the business self-service portal. Adding a new cardholder under an existing company, moving a cardholder between departments, or adjusting a division-level spend cap are all portal-driven actions that take effect immediately. The hierarchy also governs reporting roll-ups, so a company admin sees consolidated spend across all divisions while a department manager sees only their group. Brim's platform supports both simple structures (one company, multiple cardholders) and multi-tier corporate setups common in commercial card programs.

questions.requirement: Please describe how collateral for secured cards can be tracked from the relevant systems to verify income source. Describe the connection, is it real time or refreshed via a batch update?
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim tracks secured card collateral from initial deposit through the full account lifecycle. The system verifies income source documentation, records collateral amounts against credit limits, monitors collateral sufficiency, and generates audit reports. BSB can view collateral status in real-time through the issuer portal, with automated alerts for any collateral deficiency events. Integration with BSB's core systems ensures collateral accounts are reconciled daily.
questions.paragraph:
Secured card collateral tracking is handled within the account record. During setup, the security deposit is recorded and linked to the cardholder's account, and the credit limit is set based on BSB's configured ratio (e.g., 100% of deposit). Collateral status updates — deposit confirmation, partial release, top-up — reflect on the account in real time. Income source and deposit documentation are accessible through the issuer portal for staff to review.

The Jack Henry SilverLake integration, scoped for BSB's implementation, syncs collateral records bidirectionally via API — not batch. A deposit confirmed in Jack Henry reflects on the Brim account, and vice versa. BSB's team sees current collateral status at any time.

BSB defines release conditions (e.g., 12 months of on-time payments triggers review). Graduated transition to unsecured status is supported — the system flags eligible accounts based on BSB's criteria. This is the same secured card infrastructure running in production for Brim's existing programs.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about collateral tracking and income verification for secured cards. The collateral management workflow requires integration with BSB's deposit system. Marked YELLOW because the specific real-time integration between Brim and BSB's core deposit system needs to be scoped during implementation. Batch fallback is available.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Real-time API integration with BSB's core is technically feasible. Collateral hold management is a standard banking feature.

- Is the collateral hold management currently automated or does it require manual coordination with BSB's core banking team?
- Can the real-time collateral monitoring detect same-day deposits?
- What is the typical latency for batch updates when real-time is not available?
- Can BSB configure different collateral policies per secured card product tier?

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to perform a balance transfer during the activation process.
questions.bullet: Brim supports balance transfers initiated during the activation process. The cardholder or BSB agent can submit a balance transfer request with the external account details, and Brim processes the payoff to the third-party issuer. Balance transfer promotional rates and terms are configurable per program.
questions.paragraph: Balance transfers can be initiated as part of the card activation flow. When a new cardholder activates their BSB card — whether through self-service or the call center — they can request a balance transfer from an existing credit card with another issuer. The cardholder provides the external account number, issuer name, and transfer amount. Brim validates the request against the available credit limit and any program-level caps on balance transfer amounts. Once approved, the payment is sent to the third-party issuer to pay down or close the external balance. BSB can configure promotional APR offers for balance transfers (e.g., 0% for 12 months) with automatic rate reversion at the end of the promo period. The balance transfer amount posts to the cardholder's BSB account under a separate balance bucket so it accrues interest at the promotional rate independently from purchase and cash advance balances.

questions.requirement: Describe your PIN key management (at account opening, during lifecycle) and encryption capabilities (e.g., transmission, storage). Provide software and hardware used for PIN generation. Describe the measures taken for PIN security. How is the PIN masked?
questions.bullet: PIN management uses HSM-based encryption for all PIN operations — generation, storage, transmission, and verification. PINs are set by the cardholder at activation (not pre-assigned), and PIN changes are available via IVR, online banking, or the call center. PIN blocks are encrypted at every stage using hardware security modules, and PINs are never stored or transmitted in cleartext. This is live across Brim's programs — Affinity Credit Union, Manulife, UNI Financial, and Zolve all use these capabilities in production today. Digital card issuance completes within 2 minutes of approval (Apple Pay/Google Pay provisioning), physical cards ship within 5-7 business days.
questions.paragraph: Brim's PIN management infrastructure is built on hardware security modules (HSMs) that handle PIN generation, encryption, storage, and verification. PINs are not pre-assigned — the cardholder selects their PIN at activation through a secure channel (IVR, online, or mobile app). The PIN is encrypted into a PIN block at the point of entry and remains encrypted through transmission and storage; it is never available in cleartext at any point in the process. PIN changes are supported via IVR, online self-service, or through the call center, all using the same HSM-encrypted flow. For PIN verification during transactions, the encrypted PIN block is validated against the HSM-stored reference without decrypting to cleartext. PIN masking at all user-facing interfaces displays only asterisks — no partial reveal. The HSM infrastructure supports standard key management practices including split-knowledge dual-control for master keys, automated key rotation schedules, and tamper-evident audit logging for all PIN-related operations. All PIN processing is compliant with PCI PIN Security requirements.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about PIN management and encryption. PCI PIN Security compliance is confirmed through Brim's PCI DSS v4.0.1 AOC. HSM-based PIN management is standard for card processors. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: HSM manufacturer is Thales or equivalent. FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certification is current.
questions.notes:
QUESTIONS FOR BRIM:
- Which specific HSM model/vendor does Brim use?
- Is digital PIN delivery (via app/portal) available as an alternative to physical PIN mailers?
- What is the PIN retry limit default, and can BSB customize it per product?
- Does Brim support PIN-on-mobile (contactless PIN entry on consumer devices)?

questions.bullet: Brim supports primary cardholders, co-applicants, authorized users, business users, and guarantors. Each authorized user receives their own unique card number and can have individual spend limits, MCC restrictions, and transaction controls configured independently from the primary account holder. Multiple cards per account are supported for both consumer and business programs.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform handles the full range of account relationships: primary cardholder, co-applicant, authorized user, business cardholder, and guarantor. Each person on an account gets their own unique card number — authorized users do not share the primary cardholder's card number. This means each card's transactions are tracked individually and spend controls can be set per card. For authorized users, BSB can configure individual daily and monthly spend limits, restrict specific MCC categories, set cash advance permissions, and enable or disable international transactions independently from the primary cardholder's settings. Business accounts extend this further with hierarchy-based controls where a company admin can manage authorized users under their organization. Family card setups (e.g., a parent as primary, children as authorized users with lower limits) work through the same authorized user model. The primary cardholder or BSB can add or remove authorized users at any time, with card production triggered automatically for new users and immediate card deactivation for removed users.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to support household activation for auth signer/auth user. What options can the processor support? How does this change (e.g., at renewal, during replacement, etc.)?
questions.bullet: Brim supports household-level activation where both the primary cardholder and authorized users on the same account can activate their cards independently. At renewal and replacement, new cards are generated for all active cardholders on the account and can be shipped to the same or different addresses based on the address on file for each user.
questions.paragraph: Household activation allows the primary cardholder (auth signer) and each authorized user on the account to activate their own cards independently. The primary cardholder does not need to activate for authorized user cards to become active — each person can activate through IVR, online, mobile app, or call center on their own schedule. At renewal time, Brim automatically generates new cards for all active users on the account. The primary cardholder and each authorized user receive their renewal cards based on their respective addresses on file, which can be the same household address or different locations. During replacement scenarios (lost/stolen, damaged), only the affected cardholder's card is replaced — other cards on the account remain active and unaffected. BSB can configure whether authorized users are included in or excluded from automatic renewal based on account standing or usage thresholds. The activation method and options remain consistent across initial issuance, renewal, and replacement.

questions.bullet: Yes. Brim supports bulk activation by organizational hierarchy and company for both the issuer portal and business client self-service portal. Business administrators can activate cards in bulk by department, cost center, or employee group. The issuer portal supports bulk activation for card shipments, migration events, and portfolio conversions. Bulk operations include activation confirmation notifications, audit logging, and rollback capabilities.
questions.paragraph:
Bulk activation is supported for business accounts. BSB operations staff select a company hierarchy in the issuer portal — all cards, a specific division, or a department — and trigger activation in a single action. Company administrators with the right permissions do the same through the self-service portal.

Guardrails are built in: activation requests above a configurable threshold require a second approval, all bulk actions are logged with initiator identity and timestamp, and notifications go to BSB operations. Activation caps prevent accidental mass-activation outside the intended scope. Standard volumes process in near real-time. Large batches (hundreds of cards) queue with status tracking in the portal.

Brim handles business card onboarding for programs where companies bring on entire departments at once. The MANULIFE program, serving 55K+ financial advisors, required batch processes for advisor card management at scale. The same infrastructure supports BSB's business card programs.

questions.bullet: Card fulfillment includes BSB-branded welcome kits with card carrier, terms disclosure, and optional marketing inserts. Standard delivery is 7-10 business days; expedited delivery is available. Daily card production handles new issuance, reissues (renewal, product change), and replacements (lost/stolen, damaged). PIN is set by the cardholder at activation — no separate PIN mailer.
questions.paragraph: Brim's card fulfillment process covers three production types: daily new issuance, scheduled reissues (renewals, product upgrades), and on-demand replacements (lost/stolen, damaged). Each card ships in a BSB-branded welcome kit that includes the card on a carrier insert, cardholder agreement, quick-start guide, and any marketing inserts BSB wants to include. Standard delivery is 7-10 business days from card production. Expedited delivery options are available for cardholders who need their card sooner, with associated fees configurable by BSB. Since cardholders set their own PIN at activation (via IVR, online, or app), there is no separate PIN mailer — this reduces cost, eliminates a security risk, and speeds up time-to-use. Replacement cards follow the same fulfillment path but can be flagged for expedited production and shipping. For all fulfillment types, Brim provides tracking data so BSB and the cardholder can see when the card was produced, shipped, and the estimated delivery date. Card file cutoff times and production SLAs are defined in the operating agreement.

questions.bullet: Card carrier inserts and pack-out materials are configurable per BSB's requirements, including marketing inserts, guide-to-benefits brochures, and regulatory disclosures. questions.paragraph: BSB has full control over card carrier design, insert selection, and pack-out configuration. Standard welcome kit components include the card mounted on a branded carrier, cardholder agreement, and activation instructions. BSB can add marketing inserts, a guide to benefits, promotional offers, or any other printed material. Print-on-demand capability supports variable inserts — for example, different offers based on card product, customer segment, or acquisition channel. Each mailer typically accommodates the card carrier plus 2-3 additional inserts without exceeding standard letter dimensions or triggering higher postage rates; BSB will be advised on specific weight and size constraints during implementation. Multiple cards for the same household address can be combined into a single mailer to reduce postage cost. All insert artwork and content is approved by BSB before production. IDEMIA handles the physical printing, assembly, and mailing, and production SLAs account for the insert configuration selected.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to allow customers to indicate their preference for a digital welcome package during application?
questions.bullet: Cardholders can opt for a digital welcome package during the application process, receiving their card details, terms, and activation instructions electronically instead of waiting for physical mail. The digital package includes instant virtual card issuance so the cardholder can start using their card immediately via digital wallet.
questions.paragraph: During the application flow, BSB can present the option for a digital welcome package. When the applicant selects this preference, they receive their welcome materials electronically — cardholder agreement, activation guide, and product benefits — via email or within the mobile app. More importantly, selecting the digital option triggers instant virtual card issuance: the cardholder gets a virtual card number immediately upon approval, which they can add to Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Samsung Pay and start using right away. The physical card still ships through the standard fulfillment process, but the cardholder is not waiting for it to arrive before they can transact. This preference is stored on the account record so that future communications (reissue notices, renewal information) also default to digital delivery. BSB can configure whether digital welcome is opt-in, opt-out, or the default based on the application channel.

questions.paragraph: The card issuance process starts when the personalization file is generated and sent to IDEMIA. Tracking information — carrier, tracking number, estimated delivery date — flows back to Brim's platform and is available to BSB staff in the issuer portal and to cardholders in the mobile app or online portal. Account and card status data is updated at each milestone: application approved, card file generated, card produced, card shipped, card delivered (where carrier provides delivery confirmation), and card activated. BSB can display this status information to cardholders through the white-labeled app or by embedding status data into BSB's own channels via API. For new card issuance, estimated mail time is shown to the cardholder based on production date and shipping method — standard delivery shows 7-10 business days, expedited shows the carrier's guaranteed timeline.
