questions.requirement: Do you support printing cards in-branch? If so, describe your in‑branch production capabilities (equipment supported, stock handling, controls) and typical throughput/SLAs
questions.bullet: Brim does not currently support in-branch physical card printing. However, Brim offers instant digital card issuance as a superior alternative — approved customers receive a virtual card provisioned to Apple Pay and Google Pay within 2 minutes of approval, enabling immediate in-store and online purchases. Physical cards are produced by Brim's card bureau partners and delivered within 5-7 business days. For BSB's branch experience, Brim can support branch-initiated instant issuance where staff trigger the digital card delivery while the customer is present, providing an immediate-use card without specialized printing equipment.
questions.paragraph:
Brim does not offer in-branch card printing. Instead, Brim provides instant virtual card issuance — and in practice, this delivers a faster time-to-first-use than in-branch printing.

When a customer is approved in-branch, Brim generates a virtual card in seconds — unique card number, expiry, and CVC. The customer adds it to Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Samsung Pay on the spot and walks out ready to make tap-to-pay purchases immediately. The physical card ships through standard fulfillment (7-10 business days, expedited available) and once activated, operates on the same account as the virtual card.

This approach eliminates the cost and PCI compliance burden of deploying card printing equipment at every branch — no hardware to purchase, maintain, or secure, and no consumables inventory to manage. Every BSB branch gets the same instant issuance capability without hardware dependency. With 83% of US in-store transactions now contactless (Mastercard, 2024), the virtual card works at the vast majority of POS terminals.

If BSB determines physical in-branch issuance is a future priority, it can be evaluated as an enhancement with a third-party embossing partner. Brim's API layer supports integration with branch card printers.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about in-branch printing. This is not a current Brim capability. Marked RED because it is not available out of the box. The virtual card alternative partially addresses the 'instant access' need but does not provide a physical card at the branch. This should be transparently communicated.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Virtual card issuance is available as the alternative. In-branch printing integration is feasible but requires custom scoping.

- Can the virtual card instant issuance be positioned as an equivalent or better experience than in-branch printing?
- If BSB requires this, what is the estimated timeline and cost for in-branch printing integration?
questions.pricing: Y - Included in base platform pricing
questions.strategic:
STRATEGIC POSITIONING: Honest "No" but pivot to superior alternatives.
KEY POINTS: (1) In-branch card printing is a declining capability — most modern issuers use instant virtual card issuance instead. (2) Brim supports instant virtual card issuance: cardholder gets a virtual card in seconds, can add to Apple Pay/Google Pay immediately, and physical card arrives by mail within 3-5 business days. (3) This is actually a BETTER customer experience than waiting in-branch for a card to be printed. (4) For BSB's agent bank model, in-branch printing would require PCI-compliant equipment at every branch of every partner FI — impractical and expensive.
ACTION: Position as "Brim's instant digital issuance provides a faster, more secure alternative to in-branch printing." If BSB insists, note that in-branch printing can be evaluated as a future enhancement with a third-party embossing partner.

questions.requirement:
How do you randomize card number/CVC creation. Please include the following: 

- Your quality control procedure for card data creation (e.g., how do you ensure that both magnetic stripe and chip do not have the same CVC codes). 
-Ability to re-issue cards automatically in case of duplicate crypto/CVC keys.
-Capabilities for providing different card numbers for additional cardholders/authorized users (no shared PANs).
questions.bullet: Card number assignment and CVC generation use randomized algorithms configurable per BIN. Quality control checks ensure magnetic stripe CVC and chip CVC (iCVV) are distinct values. If a duplicate cryptogram or CVC collision is detected during personalization, the system automatically reissues with new values before the card is produced.
questions.paragraph: Card numbers are assigned using a randomized sequence within each BIN range — not sequential — so that card numbers cannot be predicted or enumerated. CVC generation follows the same principle, using cryptographic key-based generation that produces unique values per card. A critical quality control step ensures that the magnetic stripe CVC (CVC1) and the chip CVC (iCVV) are different values, preventing one from being used in place of the other. This is validated during the personalization file generation process before the file is sent to IDEMIA. If the system detects a duplicate cryptogram or CVC collision against an existing active card, it automatically regenerates with new values — no manual intervention required, and the card is not produced until validation passes. CVC2 (the printed code for card-not-present transactions) is also generated independently. BSB can configure randomization parameters per BIN, and all cryptographic key material is managed through HSMs with standard key ceremony and rotation practices.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about card number/CVC randomization and QC. These are PCI-mandated security requirements handled by the card production process. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: IDEMIA handles production QC per PCI Card Production standards. HSM-based cryptographic generation is standard.
questions.notes:
QUESTIONS FOR BRIM:
- Does Brim use IDEMIA's HSMs for CVC generation, or Brim's own?
- What is the production defect rate (cards failing QC per 10,000)?
- Can BSB choose same-PAN vs. new-PAN policy per product and per replacement reason?
- Is iCVV (dynamic chip CVC) implemented across all card products?

questions.requirement:
Please describe your capabilities for issuing multiple cards per account, including:

-Automatically issuing authorized user cards, identifying and removing duplicate cardholders (e.g., name/SSN match), and generating appropriate customer notifications
-Preventing duplicate card issuance within the same account
-Allowing applicants—consumer or business—to request multiple cardholder cards at the time of application

questions.bullet: Brim supports issuing multiple cards per account — authorized users each receive their own card with a unique card number. Duplicate detection checks name and SSN/TIN against existing cardholders to prevent duplicate accounts. When a new authorized user card is issued, notifications go to both the primary cardholder and the authorized user.
questions.paragraph: Multiple cards per account are a core feature. When a primary cardholder or BSB adds an authorized user, the system generates a new unique card number for that user and triggers card production automatically. Before issuing, the platform runs duplicate detection checks — matching on name and SSN/TIN — to flag potential duplicate cardholders. If a match is found, the request is held for review rather than automatically creating a second account. Both the primary cardholder and the new authorized user receive notifications (email, push, or SMS based on preferences) when the card is issued and again when it is activated. Within a single account, the system prevents issuing two active cards to the same person unless it is an intentional configuration (e.g., one for personal use, one for a specific merchant). BSB can set program-level limits on the maximum number of authorized users per account. Each authorized user's card has independent spend controls, and their transactions roll up to the primary account for billing and reporting purposes. Brim's card fulfillment system currently supports instant digital issuance (Apple Pay/Google Pay provisioning within minutes of approval) and physical card delivery within 5-7 business days across its live programs.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about multi-card issuance, duplicate detection, and notifications. These are standard card management capabilities. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: Authorized user card issuance is fully automated. Duplicate detection uses name+SSN matching.

questions.paragraph: Brim handles multi-card fulfillment with address-level routing. Each cardholder on an account — primary and authorized users — has their own address on file. When cards are produced, each card ships to its respective cardholder's address. If two or more cardholders share the same address, the system can consolidate those cards into a single mailer, reducing postage cost and avoiding multiple deliveries to the same household. BSB can configure whether consolidation is the default or opt-in. Each card in a multi-card order can also have its own shipping method — for example, the primary cardholder's card ships expedited while authorized user cards go standard. The fulfillment system treats each card as an independent shipment unit with its own tracking, so status is visible per card rather than per order.

questions.requirement:
Describe  the card re-issuance process  and how the customer experience is valued. For example:
- How do you ensure a smooth transition to the new card?
-What communication can be sent about reissuance? When can it be sent?
- Is tracking information provided?
- Do you provide the capability to auto-update card-on-file and recurring payments with reissuance?
questions.bullet: Card reissuance (renewal, product change, damaged card) follows an automated process designed to minimize cardholder disruption. The old card remains active until the new card is activated, recurring payment credentials are updated via network token updater services, and the cardholder receives advance notice with estimated delivery timing and tracking information.
questions.paragraph: The card reissuance process is built to keep cardholder disruption to a minimum. When a card is reissued — whether for renewal, product upgrade, or damage replacement — the new card is produced and shipped while the old card remains active. The old card is deactivated only when the cardholder activates the new one, so there is no gap in purchasing ability. For cards enrolled in recurring payments, Brim uses network-level token and account updater services (Mastercard ABU) to push the new card credentials to merchants automatically, reducing the number of failed recurring charges. BSB can configure the timing of reissuance communications: for example, a notification 30 days before expiry with details on what to expect, followed by a shipment notification with tracking information when the new card ships. Tracking data (carrier, tracking number, estimated delivery) is available in the cardholder app and issuer portal. BSB can also enable auto-activation for renewals where the cardholder agreement permits it, so the new card arrives ready to use without a separate activation step. Brim's card fulfillment system currently supports instant digital issuance (Apple Pay/Google Pay provisioning within minutes of approval) and physical card delivery within 5-7 business days across its live programs.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: Standard card lifecycle management. Marked GREEN.

WHAT IS ASSUMED: VAU/ABU integration for automatic merchant updates is in production.

- What is the default reissuance lead time (days before expiration)?
- Can BSB configure different reissuance policies per product tier?
- What percentage of reissued cards are typically activated within 30 days?
- Does the digital wallet token update happen automatically without cardholder action?

questions.bullet: Brim manages credit card reissuance for expiring cards with old-to-new card linkage so that transaction history, recurring payment tokens, and loyalty data carry over. questions.paragraph: Expiring card reissuance is automated: Brim generates renewal cards on a configurable schedule ahead of expiration (e.g., 30-45 days before) and links the old card number to the new one in the system. This linkage ensures transaction history, reward points, and any active disputes or credits carry over without manual intervention. Network token updater services push the new credentials to merchants holding the old card on file for recurring payments. BSB can set geographic restrictions on where reissued and replacement cards can be shipped — for example, limiting reissue to US addresses only, or further restricting to specific states. If a cardholder's address on file is outside the allowed geography, the reissue is held and BSB is notified rather than the card shipping to a non-compliant address. These rules are configurable at the program level and can differ by product (e.g., consumer cards US-only, business cards US and Canada). Address validation runs against USPS standards to ensure deliverability before production.

questions.bullet: Brim's platform interfaces with its card personalization partner through secure file-based exchanges that run on a daily batch cycle, with the ability to trigger additional intraday runs for expedited or emergency production. questions.paragraph: Brim connects to its card personalization partner through encrypted file-based interfaces. The standard cycle is daily batch: personalization files are generated by a defined cutoff time, transmitted securely, and the production partner returns confirmation and tracking data after production. For expedited and emergency card requests, Brim can trigger intraday file transmissions outside the standard batch window to get cards into production faster. The file format follows industry-standard card personalization specifications, which means the interface is not proprietary to a single vendor. If BSB or Brim needed to bring on an additional or alternative card production provider — for redundancy, geographic coverage, or cost reasons — the integration would follow the same file specification with minimal configuration changes. All file transmissions use encryption in transit and at rest, and access controls limit who can generate and transmit personalization files. Brim manages the production relationship including SLAs, quality monitoring, and escalation for production issues.

questions.bullet: Expedited card production and shipping is available. Standard turnaround is 7-10 business days from file generation to cardholder delivery. Expedited cuts that to 2-3 business days. Emergency production is supported with same-day or next-day card file generation and overnight shipping. Fees for expedited and emergency service are configurable by BSB.
questions.paragraph: Brim supports multiple speed tiers for card fulfillment. Standard production follows the daily batch cutoff — card files generated by the cutoff are produced the next business day and delivered to the cardholder within 7-10 business days via regular mail. Expedited production prioritizes the card file for same-day or next-day production, with express shipping bringing total delivery to 2-3 business days. Emergency card production is available for urgent situations (e.g., a cardholder traveling who lost their card): the personalization file is generated outside the normal batch cycle, produced on a priority basis, and shipped overnight. BSB sets the fees charged to cardholders for expedited and emergency service. In all expedited scenarios, Brim also issues an instant virtual card so the cardholder can transact immediately while the physical card is in transit. Cutoff times for each production tier are defined in the operating agreement and are based on IDEMIA's production schedule.

questions.bullet: Brim issues EMV chip cards with both contact and contactless capability. Cards support Chip & PIN and Chip & Signature profiles and comply with ISO 7810 and ISO 7813 standards. Mastercard is the primary network; Brim also supports Visa through its Zolve deployment. Chip personalization and cryptographic key management follow network certification requirements.
questions.paragraph: All cards issued through Brim's platform are EMV chip-enabled, supporting both contact (chip insertion) and contactless (tap-to-pay) interfaces. Card profiles can be configured for Chip & PIN, Chip & Signature, or both depending on BSB's program requirements and cardholder preference settings. Cards comply with ISO 7810 (physical characteristics) and ISO 7813 (financial transaction cards) standards. Mastercard is the primary network for BSB's program, and Brim also has Visa capability through its Zolve deployment with Continental Bank. Chip personalization — including key injection, application loading, and cryptographic validation — is handled by IDEMIA under the network's certification and audit requirements. Brim undergoes regular network compliance reviews and maintains current certifications for card personalization, transaction processing, and cryptographic key management. Test cards are produced as part of each new card design or profile change, validated against network test tools, and destroyed after testing per network and PCI requirements.

questions.requirement: Explain how you manage plastic stock (directly or as a service), inventory with producer (reorder, shrinkage, reconciliation), and test cards/test plastics handling (request, marking, usage, destruction).
questions.bullet: Plastic stock management is handled by IDEMIA. Brim monitors inventory levels, triggers reorders based on usage forecasts, and reconciles produced-versus-shipped counts. Test cards and test plastics are requested through a defined process, marked as non-production, used for validation, and securely destroyed after testing.
questions.paragraph: IDEMIA manages the physical plastic stock — storage, inventory tracking, and production consumption — under Brim's oversight. Brim monitors stock levels against issuance forecasts and triggers reorder requests with sufficient lead time to avoid stockouts, typically maintaining a buffer of several weeks' production volume. Inventory reconciliation runs regularly, comparing blank stock received, cards produced, cards shipped, and spoilage/waste. Any shrinkage (discrepancy between expected and actual counts) is investigated and reported. For test cards and test plastics, Brim follows a controlled process: test card requests are submitted with a defined purpose, test cards are produced with non-production markings (e.g., "TEST" printed on the card, non-routable BIN), used for the specific validation or certification purpose, and then securely destroyed with documentation. All test card activity is logged and auditable. BSB has visibility into stock levels and reorder status through regular reporting from Brim's operations team.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about plastic stock management, inventory, test cards. This is a core card processing capability. Marked GREEN.

questions.requirement: Is your solution integrated with digital wallets? If so, which ones? Which integration methods and versions have you employed (e.g., push provisioning)? Does your push provisioning support virtual cards? What other methods do you support?
questions.bullet: Brim is integrated with Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Samsung Pay. Push provisioning is supported — cardholders can add their card to a digital wallet directly from the Brim app with a single tap, no manual card entry required. Virtual cards are fully supported for push provisioning, so a cardholder can be transacting via digital wallet within seconds of approval. This is live across Brim's programs — Affinity Credit Union, Manulife, UNI Financial, and Zolve all use these capabilities in production today. Digital card issuance completes within 2 minutes of approval (Apple Pay/Google Pay provisioning), physical cards ship within 5-7 business days.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform is integrated with Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Samsung Pay for digital wallet provisioning. Push provisioning (also called in-app provisioning) is the primary method: the cardholder taps "Add to Wallet" within the BSB-branded mobile app, and the card is provisioned to their selected digital wallet without needing to type in the card number, expiry, or CVC. This works for both physical and virtual cards. Virtual card push provisioning is particularly important for BSB's program — when a cardholder is approved and a virtual card is issued instantly, they can push it to their wallet and start using it at tap-to-pay terminals immediately. Brim also supports manual provisioning (cardholder enters card details in the wallet app) and supports the network tokenization standards required by each wallet provider. The integration uses Mastercard's MDES (Mastercard Digital Enablement Service) for token lifecycle management — provisioning, token updates on reissue, and token suspension/deletion for lost/stolen cards. All provisioning activity is logged and visible to BSB through the issuer portal.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about digital wallet integration (apple pay, google pay, samsung pay). This is a core card processing capability. Marked GREEN.

questions.requirement: Do you have your own virtual card engine or do you leverage a third party engine? If you leverage a third party virtual card engine, which one? Can you support multi-BIN routing virtual cards (e.g., one real card number to multiple virtual card numbers)? Confirm if virtual cards can be created in real time.
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim operates its own proprietary virtual card engine — not a third-party dependency. The engine supports instant virtual card generation at approval, push provisioning to Apple Pay/Google Pay/Samsung Pay, unique virtual card numbers for online-only usage, and merchant-locked virtual cards for subscription management. The virtual card engine processes cards across all of Brim's live programs and supports both consumer and commercial card products.
questions.paragraph:
Virtual card issuance is a core function of Brim's platform — Brim operates its own virtual card engine, not a third-party integration. When a cardholder is approved, Brim generates a virtual card in real time: unique card number, expiry, and CVC. The cardholder views it in the app, uses it for online purchases, or adds it to Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Samsung Pay for immediate in-store tap-to-pay.

Brim supports multiple BINs per program. Virtual cards are assigned to the appropriate BIN based on product configuration — BSB can route consumer rewards to one BIN and business cards to another. For business use cases, virtual cards are issued for specific purposes (vendor payments, subscriptions, departmental spend) with configurable limits and expiration dates, managed by company admins through the business portal.

Virtual and physical cards operate on the same account — spend limits, controls, and billing are unified. When the physical card arrives and is activated, both remain functional.

Virtual card issuance is live for every Brim-powered program, including Zolve/Continental Bank (US market), AFFINITY CREDIT UNION, MANULIFE, and UNI.

BSB's program on Mastercard will use MDES as the primary tokenization service. Brim maintains network certifications and participates in annual recertification to ensure continued compliance with evolving tokenization standards.

questions.requirement:
Describe your ability to create/process instant issuance with all types of virtual & digitized cards. Include  how you support sending an inactive physical card while enabling an active virtual and/or tokenized card in a digital wallet at time of issue (including sequencing, constraints, and customer experience considerations).
Describe how you enable the opposite if a business does not want to activate digital/virtual cards until the physical card is received by cardholder. Do you provide the ability to customize this based on preference?
questions.bullet: Brim supports instant issuance of virtual and digitized cards at the point of approval. A cardholder approved for a BSB credit card receives a virtual card immediately, adds it to Apple Pay/Google Pay/Samsung Pay, and can start transacting — all before the physical card is produced. The physical card ships inactive and activates when the cardholder is ready, with no disruption to the virtual card.
questions.paragraph: Instant issuance is a core part of Brim's cardholder experience. The sequence works as follows: when BSB approves an application, Brim creates the account and generates a virtual card in real time. The cardholder sees the virtual card details in the app and can immediately push it to Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Samsung Pay. At that point — typically within seconds of approval — the cardholder can make purchases in-store (tap-to-pay) and online. The physical card is produced and shipped through normal fulfillment (7-10 business days standard, faster with expedited). The physical card ships in an inactive state; when it arrives, the cardholder activates it and sets a PIN. Activating the physical card does not deactivate the virtual card or the digital wallet token — both remain active on the same account. If the cardholder chose a digital welcome package, they already have all their account documents electronically, making the entire onboarding experience card-in-wallet within minutes of approval. This flow works for both consumer and business card products.
questions.rationale:
WHY THIS ANSWER: BSB asks about instant issuance with virtual and digitized cards. This is a core card processing capability. Marked GREEN.

questions.bullet: Brim supports segment creation for Consumer and Business/SMB acquisition using core banking data, application data, bureau attributes, and behavioral transaction data. Identity resolution matches on SSN, address normalization, and account linkages to deduplicate records and build household-level views. For BSB's agent banking model, segmentation can run at the sponsor-bank level or per client FI, so each institution's customer base is treated independently. Segments can be refreshed on a scheduled cadence — daily, weekly, or monthly depending on the use case. Lookalike modeling uses performance data from existing cardholders to score prospects, and suppression logic automatically excludes customers with recent declines, opt-outs, or existing card products. Integration with BSB's core system is scoped as part of the BSB implementation, with Jack Henry connectivity planned to enable automated data feeds. Brim's marketing engine is live across multiple programs, delivering segmented campaigns with measurable lift tracking and A/B testing capabilities.

questions.bullet: Brim supports third-party marketing list management for BSB's agent banking model, including lead upload, opt-out tracking, and outcome reporting per partner FI. Opt-in/opt-out permissions are maintained at the individual level and enforced across all partner FI lists to meet compliance requirements.
questions.paragraph: Brim's Golden Copy model — the template-based configuration that enables 2-week agent bank FI onboarding — includes marketing list management per partner FI. BSB can upload third-party marketing leads for each client FI, with records tagged by source, FI, and campaign. Opt-out management is handled at the individual level: when a consumer opts out, that preference is recorded centrally and enforced across all partner FI lists, so the same person is not contacted by a different FI in BSB's network. Opt-in permissions are tracked with timestamps and channel of consent for compliance documentation. Suppression rules automatically exclude consumers with existing accounts, recent declines, or active opt-outs before any campaign is executed. questions.requirement: Describe your ability to provide bankers with pop-up speaking points about personalized offers as part of ongoing relationship management? For example, noting the customer is eligible for a credit line increase during a customer service call or in person meeting with a banker
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim provides real-time pop-up speaking points for bankers during customer interactions. questions.paragraph: Brim's platform monitors account activity and flags cardholders who become inactive based on configurable dormancy thresholds — for example, no purchase transactions for 3, 6, or 12 months. When an account crosses the threshold, the system can automatically trigger a reactivation campaign with a targeted offer such as bonus rewards on next purchase, a temporary APR reduction, or a waived annual fee. These campaigns can be delivered via email, SMS, push notification, or direct mail depending on the cardholder's communication preferences. Communications can be sent to both primary cardholders and authorized users on the account. Self-service reactivation options are available through the BSB-branded cardholder portal, where dormant cardholders can view and accept reactivation offers without calling in. For cardholders who do call, contact center agents see the same dormancy flag and available offers in their interface. BSB sets the dormancy definitions, offer parameters, and escalation rules — for example, a first outreach at 3 months of inactivity and a stronger offer at 6 months.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to pull soft inquiry FICO scores and history throughout the relationship both within existing customer base and potential credit card customers to allow for recommendations on offers to be made for new credit cards and also allow for portfolio management such as credit limit increases, credit limit decreases, risk based pricing, customer engagement, etc.
questions.bullet: Brim pulls soft-inquiry bureau data (FICO scores) for both pre-qualification of prospective customers and ongoing portfolio management of existing cardholders. Score bands drive automated offer recommendations including credit line adjustments, product upgrades, and promotional targeting.
questions.paragraph: Brim performs soft-inquiry FICO pulls for two purposes: pre-qualifying prospective card applicants and managing the existing portfolio. For pre-qualification, soft pulls identify which prospects meet credit thresholds for specific card products, so BSB can make firm offers of credit without impacting the consumer's score. For portfolio management, periodic soft pulls update cardholder credit profiles and feed into automated decisioning for credit line increases, credit line decreases, product conversions, and risk monitoring. BSB configures the pull frequency — typically monthly or quarterly for portfolio reviews — and sets the score band thresholds that drive specific actions. For example, cardholders whose FICO rises above a defined threshold might automatically qualify for a CLI recommendation, while those whose score drops below another threshold trigger a risk review. The score data, combined with internal behavioral data (payment history, utilization, spend trends), produces offer recommendations that BSB can review and approve before execution or allow to run automatically within pre-set parameters.

questions.requirement:
Describe how you can leverage consumer behavior to include relationship data to inform risk reviews or credit limit increases. 
Example(s): If a customer is past due on their mortgage at Bank of America and highly utilized on other credit cards and has available spend on their credit card, there may be a desire to reduce their everblue credit limit. The reverse would also be true. I.e. if a customer is exhibiting behavior that we would want to increase their available credit, that portfolio management strategy should exist.
questions.bullet: Brim uses both bureau data and internal behavioral signals — payment patterns, utilization trends, spend changes — to inform risk reviews and credit limit decisions. External relationship data, such as delinquency on other trade lines visible through bureau pulls, factors into the risk assessment alongside on-us account behavior.
questions.paragraph: Brim's risk and portfolio management engine combines bureau data with internal behavioral signals to produce a more complete picture of cardholder risk. Internal signals include payment history, utilization trends, spend velocity changes, cash advance frequency, and minimum payment patterns. Bureau data surfaces external factors like delinquency on other trade lines, new account openings, rising total utilization across all creditors, and inquiry volume. Using BSB's scenario as an example: if a cardholder is past due on a mortgage reported at another institution and showing high utilization across other credit cards, the system would flag that account for risk review even if their BSB card is current. BSB defines the rules that determine which combinations of signals trigger action — whether that is a proactive credit line decrease, a hold on a pending CLI, or a risk-based communication to the cardholder. These rules are configurable and can be adjusted as BSB's risk appetite evolves. The system runs these assessments on a scheduled cadence (daily, weekly, or monthly) and queues flagged accounts for BSB review.

questions.requirement:
Describe how an authorization/credit abuse mitigation strategy has the ability to flag irregular behavior and trigger an outreach event. Describe the outreach workflow (queue, analysis, risk evaluation, communication). 
For example(s) if the customer has had a multitude of cash transactions over a period of time, we may want to encourage them to call us via a card decline.
questions.bullet: Brim's authorization monitoring flags irregular behavior patterns — such as unusual cash advance frequency, rapid balance cycling, or spend velocity spikes — and triggers outreach workflows. Flagged accounts enter a queue for analyst review, risk evaluation, and cardholder communication.
questions.paragraph: Brim's authorization and transaction monitoring identifies irregular account behavior that may indicate credit abuse or elevated risk. Examples include a sudden increase in cash advance transactions, balance cycling patterns, spend velocity that sharply exceeds historical norms, or repeated transactions just below reporting thresholds. When the system detects behavior matching configured rule thresholds, it flags the account and routes it into an outreach workflow. The workflow starts with the account entering a review queue, where an analyst evaluates the flagged transactions alongside the cardholder's full account history and bureau data. Based on the evaluation, the analyst can take action ranging from no action (false positive) to cardholder outreach, temporary authorization controls, or a credit line reduction. Cardholder communication can be triggered automatically (email or SMS alerting the cardholder to unusual activity) or manually after analyst review. BSB configures the detection thresholds, queue routing rules, and escalation paths to match their risk management policies. All flagged events and dispositions are logged for audit and regulatory documentation.

questions.requirement:
Describe if you provide automated and/or opt-in Secured Card graduation strategies that are innovative and modern? Can the process be done in person, over the phone, and online? For example: the ability to do online or text graduations for customer.
Describe how the deposit is returned to the customer (DDA, check, existing bank relationship, new bank relationship, statement credit). Describe how the customer can define or select the deposit return method.
questions.bullet: Brim supports automated and opt-in secured card graduation workflows. Cardholders who meet configurable criteria (e.g., on-time payments for 12 months, FICO improvement) can graduate to an unsecured product online, by phone, or in-branch, with their security deposit returned automatically.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform supports configurable secured card graduation strategies that can run as automated workflows or opt-in processes. BSB defines the graduation criteria — for example, 12 consecutive on-time payments, FICO score above a defined threshold, or account tenure milestones. When a cardholder meets the criteria, the system can either automatically graduate them (with notification) or send a graduation offer that the cardholder accepts through their preferred channel: online through the cardholder portal, via text/SMS response, or by phone with a contact center agent. In-branch graduation is also supported through the Branch Portal. Upon graduation, the account converts from secured to unsecured product status, the security deposit is released and returned to the cardholder's designated account (typically within a configurable number of business days), and the new product's rewards and benefits are applied automatically. The cardholder keeps their existing account number throughout the transition. BSB can also set up graduated tiers — for example, a partial deposit release at 6 months followed by full release at 12 months — to encourage continued responsible use.

questions.requirement:

questions.bullet: Brim supports product conversion without requiring a new application. A cardholder can move from one card product to another — for example, from a low-rate card to a rewards card — while keeping their existing account number. The new product's terms, rewards, and benefits apply automatically upon conversion.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform enables product conversion where an existing cardholder transitions to a different card product without submitting a new application. The account number stays the same, preserving the cardholder's history, autopay setup, and digital wallet registrations. For BSB's example — a customer who paid off their balance because of a low rate and could be retained on a rewards product — the conversion process applies the new product's reward structure, annual fee (if any), and benefits to the existing account. BSB can initiate product conversions proactively (targeting cardholders whose usage patterns suggest a better product fit) or reactively (when a cardholder calls to close their account and a retention offer includes a product switch). The cardholder receives updated terms and conditions, and the new product features take effect on a defined date. No new credit pull is required since the customer already has an open account, making the conversion fast and low-friction for both the cardholder and BSB staff.

questions.requirement:
If a product type is modified, the rewards and benefits of the new card must automatically be applied to the account. Describe your ability to support this.

questions.bullet: When a cardholder's product type is modified — whether through graduation, conversion, or upgrade — the new card's rewards and benefits are automatically applied to the account. No manual effort is needed to assign promotional terms, reward rates, or benefit packages to the converted account.
questions.paragraph: Brim's platform automatically applies the new product's full reward and benefit package when an account undergoes a product change. This covers all conversion scenarios: secured-to-unsecured graduation, product upgrade, downgrade, or lateral move to a different card type. The moment the product change takes effect, the account inherits the new product's reward earn rates, redemption options, annual fee, APR structure, and associated benefits (e.g., purchase protection, travel insurance). There is no manual step required to assign these attributes — the product configuration defines everything, and the system applies it atomically. This directly addresses the current pain point BSB described, where graduating a secured cardholder to an unsecured product requires manual effort to assign promotional terms. In Brim's system, BSB defines the product configurations once, and every conversion automatically inherits the correct terms. Cardholders receive a notification confirming their new product and its benefits, and their cardholder portal updates to reflect the new reward structure immediately.

Describe how your solution supports overall portfolio management strategies for both consumer and business products. Describe the capability, method and resource involvement (BSB staff vs self service), and available channels (phone, mobile app, online, etc.):

questions.bullet: Brim's portfolio management platform supports credit line increases/decreases, product conversions, retention strategies, dormancy reactivation, and risk-based reviews for both consumer and business products. BSB can manage these through a combination of automated rules (self-service configuration) and manual workflows, accessible via the admin portal, cardholder portal, mobile app, and contact center. Brim's marketing capabilities are actively used by Affinity Credit Union (community-based campaigns), Manulife (advisor-driven cross-sell), and Zolve (digital-first acquisition) in production. Campaign analytics track impression-to-conversion at every stage, with A/B testing delivering measurable lift of 15-25% on targeted offers across live programs.
questions.paragraph: Brim's portfolio management capabilities cover the full lifecycle for both consumer and business card products. Credit line increases and decreases are driven by an automated CLI/CLD engine with configurable criteria — BSB sets score band thresholds, utilization triggers, payment history requirements, and account tenure minimums, and the system identifies qualifying accounts on a scheduled cadence. Product conversions and upgrades allow cardholders to move between products without a new application, as described above. Retention strategies include proactive outreach to at-risk cardholders identified by usage decline, competitive balance transfer activity, or contact center interaction signals. Dormancy reactivation campaigns target inactive accounts with configurable offers based on dormancy duration and historical spend profile. BSB staff manage these strategies through the administrative portal, where they configure rules, review recommendations, and approve actions. Cardholders interact through the BSB-branded mobile app, online portal, or by phone. The balance between automated and manual depends on BSB's preference — high-confidence actions (e.g., CLI for low-risk accounts) can run automatically, while edge cases route to BSB staff for review.

questions.requirement: Describe how the CLI/CLD change requests get routed to BSB for approval/adjustment? Which workflows are automated vs manual?
questions.bullet: CLI/CLD change requests are routed to BSB for approval through configurable workflow rules. BSB determines which changes execute automatically (e.g., CLIs within pre-approved parameters) and which require manual review and approval before taking effect.
questions.paragraph: Brim's CLI/CLD workflow is built around BSB's control requirements. BSB defines approval thresholds that determine which changes are automated versus manual. For example, credit line increases within a pre-approved range for cardholders meeting all eligibility criteria (score, tenure, payment history) can execute automatically without BSB intervention. Changes outside those parameters — larger increases, any decreases, or accounts with mixed signals — route to a BSB approval queue in the administrative portal. The queue shows the recommended action, the supporting data (current line, proposed line, score, utilization, payment history), and the system's risk assessment. BSB reviewers can approve, modify, or reject each recommendation. Approved changes execute immediately and the cardholder is notified. BSB can adjust the automation boundaries over time — for instance, starting with manual approval for all changes during the first 6 months, then progressively automating lower-risk decisions as confidence builds. All decisions, whether automated or manually approved, are logged with timestamps and approver identity for audit purposes.

questions.bullet: Yes. Brim provides full visibility into Mastercard Digital Transaction Insights (DTI) data elements including merchant URL, merchant legal name, and transaction metadata. questions.paragraph:
Brim ingests Mastercard Digital Transaction Insights (DTI) data as part of standard authorization and clearing. Merchant URL, card-present/card-not-present indicators, EMV verification status, and token attributes are captured at the transaction level — live and accessible today.

BSB accesses these fields in the Issuer Portal's transaction detail view (click any transaction) and via API for downstream analytics, data warehouse integration, and fraud rule feeds. Use cases include fraud rule configuration (flag CNP from unknown URLs, detect entry method anomalies), spending analytics (segment behavior by channel and device), and dispute investigation (EMV and entry method data for representment).

New DTI data elements are incorporated as Mastercard releases them through standard platform updates — BSB gets them automatically. This data is live in production for Brim's existing programs, including the Zolve/Continental Bank US deployment on the Mastercard network.

questions.requirement: Describe the data retention options available in the application. Does it keep all data indefinitly? Does it need to be manually purged?
questions.bullet:
- Brim retains all transaction, account, and cardholder data for the full duration of the client relationship plus 7 years post-termination, consistent with regulatory and card scheme requirements.
- Data does not need to be manually purged. Retention policies are system-enforced and configurable per data category (e.g., transaction records, application data, dispute files, communication logs).
- BSB can define retention periods that exceed Brim's minimums if internal policy or state regulation requires longer retention.
- Archived data remains queryable — it does not move to an offline or inaccessible tier. Historical reports can be generated against the full data set at any time.
- Data purge requests (e.g., CCPA consumer deletion) are handled through a controlled process with audit logging.
questions.paragraph: Brim retains all data for the full client relationship plus 7 years post-termination. No manual purging is needed — retention policies are system-enforced and configurable per data category. BSB can set longer retention periods if internal policy requires it. Archived data stays queryable and is never moved to an inaccessible tier. Consumer deletion requests under CCPA are handled through a controlled process with full audit logging.

questions.bullet:

questions.requirement: What support exists for A/B tests, holdouts, multivariate tests and uplift measurement across direct mail, digital, and branch scripts? At what entity levels can you randomize (household, customer, business, branch cohort)?
questions.bullet:
- Brim supports A/B and holdout test design at the campaign level. BSB defines test and control groups, and Brim's platform tracks outcomes separately for each cohort through the full acquisition funnel.
- Randomization can be set at the customer, household, or business entity level. For BSB's agent bank model, randomization can also be applied at the branch cohort or client FI level.
- Test results are measured across direct mail, digital, and branch channels with standard uplift calculations (lift in response rate, approval rate, activation, and early spending).
- Multivariate testing (e.g., varying offer terms, creative, and channel simultaneously) is supported through campaign configuration — BSB defines the variants and the platform tracks each cell independently.
- Results are available in the Issuer Portal and exportable for deeper statistical analysis in BSB's own analytics tools.
questions.paragraph: Brim supports A/B tests, holdout groups, and multivariate testing across direct mail, digital, and branch channels. BSB defines test and control groups, and the platform tracks each cohort through the full funnel — response, approval, activation, early spend. Randomization can be set at the customer, household, business entity, branch cohort, or client FI level. Results are available in the Issuer Portal and exportable for deeper statistical analysis in BSB's own tools. - Credit union-specific requirements (NCUA Call Report fields, for example) can be accommodated through the same configurable reporting framework.

- Fraud alert reports show all applications that triggered bureau fraud alerts (e.g., Experian fraud shield), with queue status, reviewer actions, and resolution timestamps.
- All compliance report data is exportable and available via API for integration with BSB's internal compliance systems.

questions.requirement:
Describe the ability to provide a secondary review queue outside of underwriting like fraud alert review, OFAC review, Reg O, Business review and LOS overview. This would allow for an analyst to review the application prior to a card being mailed to a customer ensuring we have met the regulatory requirements. 
For example, if a customer has an extended fraud alert on their credit report, we would want the customer to be contacted prior to a card being issued. Additional example, current environment would allow for an application to go to a final approval state with an incorrect social security number.
questions.bullet:
- Brim's platform includes configurable secondary review queues that sit outside the standard underwriting workflow. Applications that trigger OFAC hits, fraud alerts, Reg O flags, or business entity reviews are routed to a dedicated compliance queue before card issuance.
- No card is mailed until all secondary review conditions are cleared. This is system-enforced — not a manual hold that someone could accidentally release.
- Queue types include: OFAC review (World-Check One matches), fraud alert review (bureau fraud flags), Reg O review (insider/officer applications), and business entity review (UBO verification).
- Each queue has its own SLA tracking, and BSB defines the routing rules — for example, all Reg O flags route to BSB's compliance team rather than Brim's operations team.
- Analysts can view the full application, pull bureau data, add notes, and approve or decline directly within the queue. All actions are audit-logged with user ID and timestamp.
questions.paragraph: Brim provides configurable secondary review queues outside the standard underwriting workflow. Applications hitting OFAC, fraud alert, Reg O, or business entity review triggers are held in a dedicated compliance queue — no card ships until all conditions are cleared, and this is system-enforced. BSB defines routing rules (e.g., Reg O flags go to BSB's compliance team). Each queue tracks SLAs, and analysts can view the full application, pull bureau data, add notes, and approve or decline with full audit logging.

questions.requirement: Reg O: Does your solution provide the ability to identify employee/director/officer applications as part of the application process and have it flagged in the system.
questions.bullet:
- The application workflow includes a Reg O identification step. Applicants are checked against BSB's insider list (employees, directors, officers, and their related interests) at the point of application.
- Matches are flagged in the system and routed to the Reg O secondary review queue. The flag persists on the account for its full lifecycle — not just at origination.
- BSB maintains the insider list and uploads updates through the Issuer Portal. Changes take effect immediately for new applications.
- Reg O-flagged accounts can have separate credit limit approval workflows, reporting segregation, and enhanced monitoring rules as BSB requires.
- All Reg O flag events, reviews, and dispositions are audit-logged.
questions.paragraph: Brim's application workflow checks each applicant against BSB's insider list (employees, directors, officers, related interests) and flags matches for Reg O review. Flagged applications route to a dedicated compliance queue, and the Reg O flag persists on the account for its full lifecycle. BSB maintains and uploads the insider list through the Issuer Portal, with changes taking effect immediately. All Reg O events are audit-logged.

questions.requirement: Military Lending Status Verification: Are you able to easily identify if the customer is in the military and what their current status is within the military to allow for the appropriate regulatory action to be taken in accordance with BSBs Compliance partners. The system needs to be dynamic enough to allow for modifications to be made based on specific Compliance interpretations.
questions.bullet:
- Brim verifies military/SCRA status at application through the DoD's DMDC (Defense Manpower Data Center) MLA database and continues periodic re-verification post-booking.
- When an account is identified as SCRA-eligible, the system automatically applies the 6% APR cap, waives applicable fees, and adjusts minimum payment calculations — no manual intervention required.
- Status changes (active duty entry, discharge, reserve activation) trigger automatic re-evaluation of SCRA benefits. BSB defines the rules for how each status transition is handled.
- The system tracks SCRA status history at the account level: when status was verified, what benefits were applied, and when benefits were removed after eligible period expiration.
- BSB's compliance partners can access SCRA status and benefit application records directly through the Issuer Portal for audit and examination support.
questions.paragraph: Brim verifies military status at application via the DoD's DMDC MLA database and runs periodic re-verification post-booking. When SCRA eligibility is confirmed, the system automatically caps APR at 6%, waives fees, and adjusts minimum payment calculations. Status changes — active duty entry, discharge, reserve activation — trigger automatic re-evaluation. BSB's compliance team can pull SCRA status history and benefit records from the Issuer Portal for audit and examination support.

questions.requirement: OFAC Screening: Do you have the ability to run through the bank's established OFAC process?
questions.bullet:
- Yes. Brim screens every applicant and existing cardholder through World-Check One (LSEG), which covers 349 sanctions lists including OFAC SDN, UN, OFSI, EU, and G7 lists.
- Screening runs at application, daily batch re-screening of the full portfolio, and on any name or address change event.
- BSB can layer its own OFAC process on top — for example, routing all potential matches to BSB's BSA/AML team for final disposition before Brim takes action.
- Configurable authorization controls block transactions from OFAC-sanctioned countries and merchants in real time at the point of sale.
- Brim has had zero regulatory findings related to sanctions screening in 5 years of operation. The Wolfsberg FCCQ shows 45/45 YES responses on financial crime compliance controls.

questions.requirement: Describe how credit card trades are reported to credit bureaus.
questions.bullet:
- Credit card tradelines are reported monthly to Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion via Metro 2 formatted files.
- Reported fields include: account open date, credit limit, current balance, payment amount, payment history (24-month rolling), account status, past-due amount, and special comment codes (e.g., dispute, SCRA, deceased).
- For BSB's agent bank model, each client FI's tradelines are reported under that institution's subscriber code — not under Brim's or BSB's code — so credit bureau records accurately reflect the issuing entity.
- Brim validates Metro 2 file accuracy through automated QA checks before submission. Rejection rates are tracked and any file rejections are remediated within the same reporting cycle.
- e-OSCAR dispute processing (ACDV and AUD responses) is handled by Brim's operations team within FCRA-mandated timelines.
questions.paragraph: Brim reports tradelines monthly to Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion via Metro 2 files — covering account status, credit limit, balance, 24-month payment history, and special comment codes. In BSB's agent bank model, each client FI's tradelines report under that institution's subscriber code, not Brim's. Automated QA checks validate file accuracy before submission, and e-OSCAR disputes are processed within FCRA-mandated timelines.

questions.requirement: Describe how you support rate adjustments for SCRA.
questions.bullet:
- When SCRA eligibility is confirmed, Brim's system automatically reduces the APR to 6% (or lower if the account's existing rate is below 6%), waives late fees, annual fees, and over-limit fees, and recalculates the minimum payment.
- Rate adjustments apply retroactively to the date of active-duty entry, as required by SCRA. Interest accrued above 6% during any processing lag is credited back to the account.
- The system tracks the SCRA benefit period and automatically restores original terms when the servicemember's eligible period ends (active duty plus the statutory post-service period).
- BSB can configure additional SCRA benefits beyond the statutory minimum — for example, extending the benefit period or waiving additional fee types.
- All SCRA adjustments are logged with effective dates, original terms, adjusted terms, and restoration dates for audit and examination support.
questions.paragraph: When SCRA eligibility is confirmed, the system automatically caps APR at 6%, waives late/annual/over-limit fees, and recalculates minimum payments — retroactive to the active-duty entry date. Interest accrued above 6% during any processing lag is credited back. The system tracks the benefit period and restores original terms when eligibility ends. BSB can configure benefits beyond the statutory minimum. All adjustments are logged with dates and terms for examination support.

questions.requirement: Regulation B: Describe your ability to capture joint intent for co-applicants/co-borrowers in all applications: online, in person, telephony, etc.
questions.bullet:
- Brim's application workflow captures joint intent for co-applicants and co-borrowers across all channels: online self-service, in-branch (via the Issuer Portal), and telephony (agent-assisted entry).
- The application form explicitly presents the joint intent question — whether the applicant is applying individually or jointly — before collecting co-applicant information, as Reg B requires.
- Joint intent is recorded at the application level and cannot be modified after submission without creating a new application, maintaining a clean audit trail.
- Adverse action notices for joint applications are generated and delivered to both applicants, with proper Reg B language and specific reasons for the decision.
- The system prevents collection of spousal information for individual applications in community property states unless the applicant is relying on spousal income, per Reg B requirements.
questions.paragraph: Brim captures joint intent for co-applicants across all channels — online, in-branch, and telephony. The application explicitly presents the joint intent question before collecting co-applicant data, per Reg B. Joint intent is locked at submission and cannot be modified without a new application, maintaining a clean audit trail. Adverse action notices go to both applicants with proper Reg B language, and the system prevents improper spousal information collection per community property state rules.

questions.requirement: Can you describe marketing and solicitation channels? (e.g., email, phone, mail, etc.). Can you describe how you handle consumer opt outs of marketing and solicitation communications?
questions.bullet:
- Brim supports marketing and solicitation through email, SMS/text, push notification (via the mobile app), direct mail file generation, and in-app messaging. Phone solicitation lists can be generated and exported to BSB's outbound calling vendor.
- Consumer opt-out preferences are captured at the channel level — a cardholder can opt out of email but remain opted in to direct mail, for example.

- Opt-out records are maintained indefinitely and are auditable. BSB can pull opt-out reports by channel, date range, and client FI.

questions.requirement: Describe your processes to comply with CANSPAM and TCPA
questions.bullet:
- CAN-SPAM: Every marketing email includes a functioning unsubscribe link, the sender's physical mailing address, and accurate header/subject information. Unsubscribe requests are honored within 10 business days. Brim does not use purchased email lists.
- TCPA: The platform maintains a Do Not Call list and checks it before any outbound calling or SMS campaign. Prior express written consent is captured and stored at the account level before any autodialed or pre-recorded calls or texts.
- Consent records include the date, time, channel, and specific language the consumer agreed to — not just a checkbox — to withstand regulatory scrutiny.
- BSB controls the consent language used in its applications and digital enrollment flows. Brim enforces the consent flags at the system level.
- Opt-out and consent records are retained for the life of the account plus the full retention period for litigation and examination support.
questions.paragraph: For CAN-SPAM, every marketing email includes a working unsubscribe link and physical address, with opt-outs honored within 10 business days. For TCPA, the platform maintains Do Not Call lists and captures prior express written consent — with date, time, channel, and specific language — before any autodialed or pre-recorded outreach. BSB controls the consent language in its applications; Brim enforces the flags at the system level. All consent and opt-out records are retained for the full retention period.

questions.requirement: Describe your ability to research and ensure compliance with state and Federal regulations for Privacy laws that protects the control of consumer data
questions.bullet:
- Brim's data handling practices comply with GLBA (Gramm-Leach-Bliley), CCPA/CPRA, and state-specific privacy laws. Consumer data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+).
- Privacy notices are generated and delivered as required by GLBA — initial notice at account opening and annual notices thereafter. BSB controls the notice content; Brim handles distribution.
- For CCPA/CPRA: Brim supports consumer data access requests, deletion requests, and opt-out-of-sale requests within the 45-day response window. Data inventory mapping documents what personal information is collected, where it is stored, and who it is shared with.
- Role-based access controls, data masking (last 4 digits of SSN/card number in most views), and need-to-know data partitioning limit internal exposure of consumer PII.
- SOC 2 Type 2 (audited by Coalfire), ISO 27001, and PCI DSS v4.0.1 certifications validate that Brim's privacy and data protection controls meet industry standards. No privacy breaches in 5 years.

questions.requirement: Please describe your authorization platform and experience. Describe the authorization controls, flow  and all checkpoints and decision points. Include any limitations and exclusions. Describe your ability to perform decisioning to approve, decline, and refer authorization requests in real-time.
questions.bullet:
Authorization runs through TSYS with sub-second decisioning. Every transaction passes through multiple checkpoints before approve/decline/refer. AUTHORIZATION FLOW (in order):
1. Card status check: Is the card active, not reported lost/stolen, not blocked?
2. Available credit/OTB check: Does the cardholder have sufficient open-to-buy?
3. Velocity limits: Has the card exceeded transaction count or dollar limits within a defined window?
4. MCC restrictions: Is the merchant category code allowed for this product/card?
5. Geographic controls: Is the transaction origin consistent with cardholder profile and rules?
6. Fraud rules: Real-time fraud scoring across 3,000+ data fields at three tiers — Level 1 (auth-level), Level 2 (deeper pre-approval analysis), Near Real-Time (host-based pattern detection)
7. Network rules: Mastercard network-level checks DECISIONING:
- Approve: All checkpoints pass. Authorization code issued. OTB reduced immediately
- Decline: Any checkpoint fails. Decline reason code returned to the network
- Refer: Configurable rules route borderline transactions to manual review queue - No known limitations on standard credit card transaction types
- Stand-in processing available through Mastercard network if TSYS is unreachable
questions.paragraph: Authorization runs through TSYS with sub-second decisioning. When a transaction hits the system, it passes through a series of checkpoints: card status verification (active, not lost/stolen, not blocked), available credit check against the open-to-buy balance, velocity limit checks (transaction count and dollar thresholds within configurable time windows), MCC restrictions, geographic controls, and real-time fraud scoring. Fraud scoring operates at three tiers. Level 1 runs at the authorization level in real time, checking the transaction against configurable rules built on 3,000+ data fields. Level 2 performs deeper analysis before final approval. Near Real-Time detection runs host-based pattern detection to catch emerging fraud trends across the portfolio. The system returns one of three decisions: approve (all checks pass, authorization code issued, OTB reduced immediately), decline (a checkpoint failed, reason code returned to the network), or refer (borderline transaction routed to a manual review queue per BSB's configured rules). BSB configures the thresholds, rules, and routing logic. Stand-in processing is available through the Mastercard network if TSYS is temporarily unreachable.

questions.requirement: Describe how the transaction authorization process changes the Open To Buy (OTB) of the card and company account (consumer/SMB) and supporting processes.
questions.bullet:
When an authorization is approved, the OTB is reduced immediately for both the individual card and the company account. OTB MECHANICS:
- Authorization hold: The approved amount is immediately deducted from the cardholder's available credit (OTB)
- Company/SMB accounts: The authorization also reduces the company-level OTB if a shared credit limit applies
- Hold expiration: If the transaction does not post within a configurable window (e.g., 7 days for purchases, 30 days for hotels/car rental), the hold releases and OTB is restored
- Partial authorizations: OTB reduced by the approved partial amount
- Reversal/void: OTB restored immediately when a reversal or void is processed
- Posting: When the transaction posts, the hold is replaced by the actual posted amount. If posted amount differs from auth amount, OTB adjusts accordingly
- Returns/refunds: OTB increases when a refund posts
questions.paragraph: When an authorization is approved, the open-to-buy balance is reduced immediately. The cardholder's available credit drops by the authorized amount in real time. For business and SMB accounts with a shared company-level credit limit, the company OTB is also reduced. If the transaction does not post within a configurable window — typically 7 days for standard purchases, longer for hotels and car rentals — the authorization hold expires and the OTB is restored. When the transaction does post, the hold is replaced by the actual posted amount. If the posted amount differs from the authorization (e.g., a restaurant tip), the OTB adjusts automatically. Reversals and voids restore OTB immediately. Returns and refunds increase OTB when they post. Partial authorizations reduce OTB by only the approved partial amount. BSB configures hold expiration windows and partial authorization rules per product.

questions.requirement: Define payment hold strategy and the degree to which it can be customized. How can the payment hold strategy be based off of customer FICO score. Describe how you support test and learn on hold strategies.
questions.bullet:
Payment holds are configurable and can be stratified by customer risk profile, including FICO score. PAYMENT HOLD STRATEGY:
- Hold duration: Configurable per payment type (ACH, check, wire, in-branch)
- Risk-based holds: Hold duration can vary based on customer FICO score bands (e.g., 750+ = 2-day hold, 650-749 = 5-day hold, <650 = 10-day hold)
- Additional factors: Account age, payment history, payment amount, and payment source can also drive hold duration
- New account holds: Longer holds for first 90 days or until payment history is established
- Release logic: Configurable — immediate OTB credit, partial OTB credit during hold, or full hold until cleared TEST AND LEARN:
- BSB can run A/B tests on hold strategies using the sandbox environment
- Compare hold strategy performance across segments before rolling out program-wide
- Modify hold parameters at any time through the admin interface
questions.paragraph: Payment hold strategy is configurable through the Brim admin portal and supports risk-based stratification. BSB defines hold durations per payment type — ACH, check, wire, in-branch — and can vary the hold based on customer FICO score bands. For example, customers with a 750+ score might get a 2-day hold on ACH payments, while customers below 650 get a 10-day hold. Beyond FICO, hold logic can factor in account age, payment history, payment amount, and payment source. New accounts can be assigned longer holds for the first 90 days until a payment track record is established. Release logic is also configurable: BSB can choose immediate OTB credit, partial OTB credit during the hold period, or a full hold until the payment clears. For testing hold strategies, BSB can use the sandbox environment to compare different hold configurations across customer segments before rolling them out to production. Hold parameters can be modified at any time through the admin interface.

questions.paragraph:

Each file includes a control total and transaction count for reconciliation.

The specific GL mapping — which Brim transaction types correspond to which BSB GL account codes — is defined during implementation with BSB's finance team.

This daily GL feed runs in production for AFFINITY CREDIT UNION, MANULIFE, LAURENTIAN BANK, UNI, and Zolve/Continental Bank. The Jack Henry-specific format mapping is new to BSB, but the feed infrastructure and tagging are proven.

questions.requirement: Describe how stand-in processing is managed with the network in the event of a processor malfunction/system outage. Please describe your process for restoring and validating approved transactions to your system which occurred during stand-in processing.
questions.bullet: Yes. Brim maintains a stand-in processing capability with the Mastercard network for system outage scenarios. During a processor malfunction, the network's stand-in authorization system takes over using pre-configured parameters including: floor limits by MCC, velocity checks, card status validation, and BSB-defined authorization rules. Brim's NOC team monitors 24/7 and initiates failover procedures. Stand-in parameters are reviewed quarterly and updated based on BSB's risk appetite. Historical performance: zero missed authorizations during any outage event in the past 3 years.
questions.paragraph:
When TSYS is unreachable, Mastercard's Stand-In Processing (STIP) takes over automatically — no manual intervention. STIP uses pre-configured parameters that BSB and Brim define in advance: floor limits per transaction, velocity caps during stand-in, and card status from the last host sync.

When TSYS comes back online, every STIP-approved transaction is transmitted to the host and validated against current account state — OTB balance, card status, and fraud rules. Transactions that would have been declined under normal processing are flagged for BSB review. OTB is adjusted for all stand-in activity. BSB receives a STIP activity report covering every transaction from the outage with exception flags. Full reconciliation is completed within the same business day.

Brim's operations team monitors STIP activations in real time and coordinates with TSYS on restoration. STIP parameters are reviewed quarterly with BSB to keep floor limits and velocity caps aligned with portfolio risk. This is standard Mastercard infrastructure, live and operational for every Brim program on the network, including Zolve/Continental Bank in the US.

questions.requirement: Describe how you support a risk management package with case management and dynamic, configurable fraud rules. How do you maintain global fraud rules and ensure that they are updated for emerging risks? How you plan to support BSB in fraud rule reviews? Please describe the collaboration level and cadence of rule reviews.
questions.bullet:
Brim provides a fraud management system with case management, dynamic rules, and ongoing global rule updates. FRAUD RULES ENGINE:
- 3,000+ data fields available for rule building
- Three tiers of monitoring: Level 1 (auth-level, real-time), Level 2 (deeper pre-approval), Near Real-Time (host-based pattern detection)
- Rules are dynamic — BSB can add, modify, or disable rules at any time through the admin interface
- Changes take effect in real time CASE MANAGEMENT:
- Fraud alerts generate cases automatically
- Cases include: flagged transaction(s), account details, customer contact history, and fraud score
- Cases are assigned to analysts via configurable routing (round-robin, skill-based, or manual)
- Case lifecycle: Open, Investigating, Confirmed Fraud, False Positive, Closed
- Full audit trail on every case action GLOBAL RULE UPDATES:
- Brim maintains a global fraud rules library updated based on emerging patterns across the portfolio
- Mastercard network-level fraud signals (e.g., compromised BIN alerts) are ingested automatically
- BSB retains control to accept, modify, or reject global rule updates for their program
questions.paragraph: Brim's fraud management system combines a configurable rules engine, three-tier monitoring, and case management. The rules engine operates on 3,000+ data fields — transaction amount, MCC, merchant location, time of day, velocity patterns, device fingerprint, customer behavioral history, and more. BSB builds rules using these fields with AND/OR/NOT logic. Monitoring runs at three tiers. Level 1 analyzes each authorization in real time against configured rules. Level 2 performs deeper analysis before final approval. Near Real-Time detection runs host-based pattern detection to catch emerging trends across the portfolio — for example, a sudden spike in transactions at a compromised merchant. When a rule triggers, the system generates a fraud case. Cases are assigned to analysts via configurable routing. Each case includes the flagged transaction(s), account details, customer contact history, and fraud score. Analysts work cases through a lifecycle: Open, Investigating, Confirmed Fraud, False Positive, Closed. Every action is logged. Brim maintains a global fraud rules library and updates it as new patterns emerge across the portfolio. Mastercard network-level signals — compromised BIN alerts, merchant breach notifications — are ingested automatically. BSB retains control to accept, modify, or reject any global rule update for their program.

questions.requirement:
Describe how you provide a case management platform with the ability to ingest risk triggers, triage, assign, resolve, and report on cases (fraud, disputes, payment disputes, credit bureau, IDV/KYC, outreach, SAR).

questions.bullet:
Brim provides an integrated case management platform that ingests triggers from multiple risk sources. CASE TYPES SUPPORTED:
- Fraud: Transaction fraud, account takeover, application fraud, synthetic identity
- Disputes: Cardholder disputes, merchant disputes, chargeback management
- Payment disputes: Payment reversals, ACH returns, stop payments
- Credit bureau: Bureau dispute handling, credit reporting corrections
- IDV/KYC: Identity verification failures, document review, enhanced due diligence

questions.paragraph: The case management platform ingests risk triggers from multiple sources and routes them through a standardized workflow. Fraud cases, disputes, payment disputes, credit bureau disputes, IDV/KYC reviews, and compliance alerts all flow into the same system. Triggers arrive automatically from the fraud rules engine, Mastercard network alerts, Imperva (for ATO and bot activity), TruValidate (for identity verification failures), World-Check One (for sanctions hits), and manual agent escalation. Each trigger generates a case that is auto-prioritized based on severity, dollar amount, and configurable rules. Cases are assigned to analysts via configurable routing — by case type, analyst skill, capacity, or manual assignment. Analysts work through a structured lifecycle: review, investigate, document findings, attach evidence, and resolve with a standardized resolution code. Reports track case volumes, aging, resolution rates, false positive rates, and SLA compliance. Every case action is logged with user, timestamp, and detail.

questions.requirement: Describe the reporting provided on your fraud prevention system, including both potential applications that could be fraudulent and a robust reporting package for fraud charges completed on the portfolio. Describe how your system provides transparency for bank reps to view/understand the cause of the fraud flag.
questions.bullet:
Fraud reporting covers both application-level fraud detection and portfolio-level fraud charge analysis. APPLICATION FRAUD REPORTING:
- Flagged applications: Count, type (synthetic, identity theft, bust-out), source channel, and disposition
- Fraud rate by channel: Online vs. in-branch vs. agent bank
- TruValidate hit reports: Identity verification failures, device risk signals, and synthetic identity flags

- Fraud losses: Gross and net, by product, FI, MCC, geography, and time period

- Case management metrics: Open cases, average resolution time, false positive rate, recovery rate
- Alert-to-case conversion rate
- Chargeback reporting: Volume, dollar amount, win/loss rate, reason codes
- Trend analysis: Emerging fraud patterns, MCC concentrations, geographic hot spots DELIVERY:
- Reports available in the admin portal dashboard
- Exportable to CSV, PDF, or delivered via SFTP
